BIRTHDAY ANNIVERSARY OF ONE OF
OUR OLDEST VETERANS.

	Thursday June 10th a few friends assembled
at the house of our old and long tried friend Sis-
ter Vienna Jacques to offer her congratulations
upon her arriving at her 94th natal day.
	A number of distinguished ladies intended
paying her their respects, but duty called them
elsewhere.

	The old lady's apparent lease of life and vigor
is remarkable still and bids fair to give us all the
rare privilege of beholding one having lived an
hundred years.

	The erectness of her carriage is sufficient to fill
many of the Misses of the nineteenth century
with envy. May she live as long as she desires,
and may the angel of the Lord preserve her from
all evil both seen and unseen is the prayer of all
her numerous friends.				R. B. P.


EDITORIAL NOTES.

	The Sisters' Stake Conference in Tooele Count-
ty was held on Saturday and Sunday, June 12th
and 13th. Mrs. Rachel R. Grant, of Salt Lake
City, attended the meetings.

	Mesdames M. Isabella Horne and S. M. Hey-
wood visited South Jordan and several other
Wards in this county in the interest of sisters'
organizations, June 10th, 11th and 12th.

	WE have received newspaper notices in full
almost of the Convention of the N. W. S. A.
held in Indianapolis, Ind., May 26th and 27th,
but we cannot find room at present for more
than the resolutions adopted by the convention.

	THE Primary Hymn Book for the children,
edited by Eliza R. Snow Smith, is now in the
hands of the publishers. It will contain 128
pages, and will, it is expected, be sold so reason-
ably that all the children in the Territory can
have one.

	THE air is heavy laden with the breath of
June roses, fragrant honeysuckle and other deli-
cious perfumes. These with the charming sun-
sets, the clear sparkling rills, and other delight-
ful features of the scenery in this sequestered
vale in the "tops of the mountains," gladden the
heart and refresh the soul.

	WHO is to be b e the next President of the
United States? is the popular question now agi-
tating the public mind. The nomination of
General Garfield by the Republican party has
taken the people by surprise, so also, perchance,
may the nomination of the Democratic candi-
date. There are many surprising as well as won-
derful things happening nowadays, and none can
tell what a day may bring forth.


WOMAN'S CONVENTION.

	The National Woman Suffrage Association
held its annual meeting May 26th and 27th, ac-
cording to appointment, in Indianapolis, Indiana.
In absence of the President, Mrs. Stanton, Miss
Anthony, Vice-President-at-large presided. The
ladies were earnest in their work and system-
atic in the manner of conducting the business of
the convention.
	Mrs. Gage, chairman of committee on resolu-
tions, reported the following platform and reso-
lutions:
	The National Woman Suffrage Association is
an organization with the special object of secur-
ing national protection for women citizens in the
exercise of the right of suffrage. It recognizes
the fact that our government was framed on the
political basis of the consent of the governed as
the security of all rights, and that the Declara-
tion of Independence struck a blow at every ex-
isting form of government, by declaring the in-
dividual to be the source of all power, upon
which newly proclaimed truth .. our nation rose.
The members of this association, outside of our
great question, hold diverse political beliefs and
affiliations, but for the purpose of gaining their
great right to the ballot, its members hold their
party predilections in abeyance; therefore,
Resolved, That in this year of presidential
nominations and political campaigns, we an-
nounce our determination to support no party by
whatever name called, unless such party shall
in its platfo[-]m first, emphatically endorse; and
W[-]ERE[-]S, Self-government carries - with it
the right to examine and control all great re-

[Column 2]
formatory and governmental questions as shown
in the United States, by the use of the ballot,
which has raised us in influence and power to
the first rank as a nation, and rendered us the
hope of all oppressed, down-trodden people of the
earth; broadening and developing all grades of
native and foreign born men, in whose hand the
ballot has been placed; and
	WHEREAS, The interests and welfare of both
men and women in all the relations of life are
identical, and whatever affects one class, for
either good or evil, also affects the other in the
same manner and in the same proportion; there-
fore,
	Resolved, That what the ballot has done for
man, native and foreign-born, it is equally capa-
ble of doing for woman, and we demand it as a
means of self-culture as well as a security for
our inherent rights of self-government.
	WHEREAS, The era of force is rapidly giving
way to the era of intellectual and moral power,
as shown in the substitution of arbitration for
war; in the proposed disarmament of Europe; in
the great industrial expositions of various coun-
tries, which bring the people of different nations
into acquaintance and harmony; in the spread of
commerce touching every distant and savage
tribe; in the explorations which leave no part of
the globe untrod; in the rapid strides of the rail-
road and telegraph; in the advance of science;
in the general diffusion of knowledge; and
	WHEREAS, The opinions of women are power-
ful upon these questions; and
	WHEREAS, The Woman Suffrage movement
does not arise from personal grievances, but is a
question of human right in exact union with the
announced principles of our government; there-
fore,
	Resolved, That we demand the practical appli-
cation of those principles to the women citizens
of the nation, who not only compose one-half of
the people, but who are acknowledged to pre-
eminently carry with them the great moral
forces which are now so rapidly coming to gov-
ern the world; thus securing responsible recog-
nition of woman's opinions upon all the great
questions which rise for discussion and settle-
ment.
	Resolved, That the present stage of the Wo-
man Suffrage movement is practical, and the
duty of Woman is to take the position and as-
sume the responsibility of citizenship as far as is
possible
	Resolved, That republican government depends
for stability upon the full representation and en-
lightenment of all citizens, and the people, wo-
men as well as men, can safely be trusted to re-
present themselves.
	Resolved, That we hail with delight the ad-
vance of public sentiment among all classes in
favor of Woman's enfranchisement, and es-
pecially do we view with profound thankfulness
the growing conviction in the minds of "Chris-
tian temperance women" of their need of the
ballot, and of the duty and responsibility rest-
ing upon them to labor for its possession in
order that they may more effectively work for
those reforms in legislation which so vitally
concerns the welfare of their homes, and of so-
ciety.
	Elizabeth Boynton Harbert, of the Chicago
Inter-Ocean recited her poem entitled, "The
Coming Man." Addresses were made by
Mesdames Sara Andrews Spencer, Matilda
Joslyn Gage, Lillie Devereux Blake, Miss Mary
B. Clay and Mrs. E. L. Saxon.
	The following resolutions were also presented
on the second day:
	Resolved, That human rights are inherent in
human nature, and the inalienable right to life
and liberty can never be rightfully surrendered
to majorities, in despotisms, nor in republics, nor
dependent on the will of any government. And
so freedom is not less a duty and obligation than
a right.
	Resolved, That the right to see comes with
the eye and the sight; as the right to breathe
with the lungs and the air, and the right to lib-
erty is no less divinely guaranteed to every
human being possessing the qualities and attri-
butes necessary to constitute an intelligent,
moral and responsible being.
	Resolved, That no man was ever born with
three ballots in his hand—one for himself as a
birthright, the other to be given, as a boon, to
his wife or daughter, or withheld as his caprice,
convenience, or despotic choice might dictate.

[Column 3]
And any such claim by man, or a government
of men, is highhanded robbery to be resisted at
whatever cost.
	Resolved, That taxation without representa-
tion is no less tyranny to-day than a hundred
years ago; is even more tyrannical when im-
posed on women than it could have been when
suffered at the hand of a distant and despotic
government of our revolutionary ancestors.
	Resolved, That while the women of Massa-
chusetts are favored by careful computation, to
be paying annually one-eleventh part of all the
taxes imposed on that Commonwealth, and the 
women of Connecticut nearly if not quite the
same, we can but approve and admire the devo-
tion to principle of Abby Kelly Foster, of the
former, and Julia and Abby Smith, of the lat-
ter State, for refusing voluntary payment of
such cruelly and unjustly levied impositions,
though always at great pecuniary sacrifice and
loss. Nor can any reason be given why. Had
they a General Lafayette to come to the rescue,
as to the aid of George Washington in the war
of 1775, their own homesteads should not be
made the Lexington and Bunker hill of another
revolution, the most righteous war recorded in
the history of the human race.
	Resolved, That the rapid advance in public
sentiment and State legislation, upon the rights
of women, and the respect shown by Congress,
indicate that our demands are in harmony, with
the spirit of the age, and that the political party
that opposes the civil and political equality of
women will have a brief lease of power.
	Resolved, That granting to women the right
of Suffrage in so many states and municipalities
on questions of education, and the means and
methods for suppressing the frightful evils of in-
temperance, has forever answered all questions,
principles or expediency as to Woman's fitness
and capability to exercise that right; and we
hold it as most undeniable that if Woman
shonld be permitted to vote for laws to protect
her sons from drunkenness, she should have
equal voice in legislation to preserve her daugh-
ters from those dreadful dangers to which they
are every day becoming more and more ex-
posed through the unbridled and insatiable lust
of men. Nor has the law-making power to-day
one single valid reason to urge w[--]y women
should not be permitted to exercise all the
rights, and assume at once all the responsib-
ities of equal citizenship.
	Resolved, That the thanks of this association
are hereby tendered to the Republican party in
Congress, for requesting the national committee
to provide seventy-six seats for women delegates
in the Chicago presidential convention.
	And our thanks are especially due to Hon.
Thomas W. Ferry, of the Sentate, and to Hon.
Edwin Willetts, of the House of Representa-
tives, for advocating this provision for women.
	Resolved, That we are a nation and not a con-
federacy of states; that we are all citizens first of
the United States, and second of the States
wherein we reside. Hence the right of self-
government should be guaranteed by the nation-
al constitution to all citizens, and should be
protected by the ballot everywhere under our
flag.
	Whereas, The women of the land, defrauded
of their earnings and of their personal liberty,
can not educate the 20,000,000 of men concern-
ing their rights as citizens; and
	Whereas, Whenever State legislatures have
submitted our rights to the rank, and file of the
men of a State, the solid liquor interest and the
kindred forces combine to defeat us; and
	Whereas, To enfranchise the women of one
State does not establish woman's rights as a
United States citizen to vote; therefore,
	Resolved, That it is the duty of Congress to
submit to the legislatures of the several States
an amendment to the national constitution,
securing women citizens the right of suffrage.
	Whereas, A nation's chief wealth does not con-
sist alone in its material resources, but also its
love of justice, and its desire to secure equality of 
opportunity and rights to all; therefore,
	Resolved, That corruption in politics, the
decline of patriotism, the lack of justice and the
prevalence of sentiment of disloyalty, which now
endangers the life of the republic are traceable
to withholding from women all participation in
the affairs of government, and since the child
is influenced by the condition of the mother and
its first training comes from her, therefore wo-
man's absolute freedom from all restraint upon
her powers, as well as her endowment with all
the rights of citizenship, is not only imperative-
ly necessary for the perpetuation of our free in-
stitutions, but for the securing of a higher type
of humanity.


14		WOMAN'S  EXPONENT.


RELIEF SOCIETY REPORTS.

	A brief report of Portage Relief Society, Box
Elder County. We held our annual meeting on
the 15th of May. The following officers were
elected and unanimously sustained: Mrs. Lu-
cinda Hoskin, President; Mrs. Julia A. Gibbs
and Mrs. Margaret John, Counselors; Jane H.
John, Secretary; Eliza A. Hall, Asst. Sec'y;
Jane Hall, Treasurer. There are about forty
members enrolled, we meet every two weeks
and enjoy a goodly portion of the Spirit of the
Lord. Our society is in a flourishing condition,
our meetings are well attended and we are often
blest with the encouraging remarks of our worthy
Bishop, O. C. Hoskin and his assistants, and we
feel like doing all in our power to held build up
the Kingdom of God. The sisters of our Society
are trying to carry out the counsel of the autho-
rities of the Church in storing grain, for we
know it will be our salvation in days to come.
Our crops for the last two or three years have
been cut off by frost, grasshoppers and crickets,
and it is rather a hard matter to lay up grain
where there is none, but we intend to try, try
again; and hope in time to succeed. The breth-
ren are now at work on a water ditch, which,
when completed, will be a great benefit to this
settlement, as water is very scarce in this part of
the valley. We have a week day and Sunday
School, a Young Men's and Young Ladies' Mu-
tual Improvement Association. We are also
called upon to donate means to assist in build-
ing the Temple in Logan, and it is our desire to
re[-]pond to every call made by the servants of the
Lord. In conclusion, I will say that the Expo-
nent is an ever welcome visitor and is highly ap-
preciated; we read it in our meetings and are
greatly benefitted by its valuable instructions.
May God bless the noble women that spend their
time and talents in helping to roll forth this
mighty work, is the prayer of
			Your sister in the Gospel,
					ELIZA A. HALL, Sec'y.

BEAVER.

	The first Ladies conference of the Beaver
Stake of Zion convened at Beaver in the meet-
ing house, May 14th, 1880. Present on the
stand were the several presidents and counselors
of the several branches of this Stake President
Lucinda Houd presided over the Conference.
President John Ashworth and several other
brethren met with the sisters and manifested a
liberal disposition to aid the ladies in their good
work.
	Reports of the different branches were handed
in. The societies of the Y. L. M. I. were well
represented, as also was the Primary Associa-
tion of the Beaver Branches. Two days were
spent by the sisters in this interesting capacity.
	Statistical and financial report of Beaver
Stake for the year ending May 14th, 1880. Mem-
bers, total, 207; real estate, $2,822.50; capital
stock in co-op. store, $100; wheat loaned out,
274 bushels; disbursements to the poor, $30.80.
			FLORA S. HILL, Stake Secretary.


[Column 1]
PRIMARY REPORTS.

	The first annual meeting of the Primary As-
sociation of Samaria was held Thursday evening,
May 6th, 1880. A large audience convened—
several of the Priesthood of the ward were
among the number. The evening entertain-
ment consisted of songs, recitations and dia-
logues. Six of the little ones, under ten years
of age, gave a very nice dialogue on Spring, four
others gave a dialogue entitled, "The Bound
Girl," and one little girl, eight years old read
the chapter from the New Testament, which
speaks of the widow's mite, and, although the
house was crowded every word was distinctly
heard to the other end of the room. We have
also made a commencement in the straw busi-
ness and several of the little girls have learned
to braid straw and they wear hats braided by
their own hands, and I am happy to say that
several of the children have taken Sister Eliza
Snow's counsel—when they had five cents
given them they have put it into the Association
and by that means have raised enough to buy
blocks for the straw they braid. they are
anxious to attend their meetings, and when I
am unable to attend through sickness, the old-
est of the little girls will carry on the meeting,
and feels glad to do so, and when the minutes of
that meeting have been read I have been truly
gratified to hear them. I often look back to the
time they were organized and feel grateful to

[Column 2]
my Heavenly Father for the progress they have
made. It is true it seems slow work with the
little ones while laboring with them, but I feel
thankful to the Lord for the assistance of his
Holy Spirit enabling us to persevere in this
great work, realizing the time will come when
these little ones will stand forth battling for
truth against error; then will their petitions as-
cend to God our Heavenly Father for blessings
upon those that organized them and those who
have labored with them, teaching them the
laws of God, preserving them from wolves in
sheeps' clothing who seek to draw the young
from the fold, and who try to bring destruction
upon the people of God. To mothers I would
say, encourage your little ones to attend their
meetings, attending them also yourselves; do
not think it too much trouble, they will repay
you in the future, becoming bright and shining
lights, crowned with immortality and eternal
lives: may our Heavenly Father bless all that
labor with the young and rising generation is
the prayer of your co-laborer,
							AMY DAVIS.

RICHMOND.

	Yearly report of the Primary Association of
Richmond, beginning June 1st, 1879, and ending
June 1st, 1880: Received in donations from mem-
bers, $3.90; Received January 1st, 1880, on pro-
ceeds of Primary dance, $9.00. Disbursements:
Paper and pencil, 20c.; Paper, 20c.; Candy for
children, $2.35; To poor, $3.50; Cards for mem-
bers. $3.15; Total amount received, $12.90; Total
amound disbursed, $9.40; Remaining in treasury,
$3.50. Our yearly concert was well attended
and a good spirit prevailed. The children enter-
tained us with a choice selection of songs, reci-
tations and dialogues, which were weli prepared
for the occasion. I must make mention of two
little boys of Brother Monson's, one six and the
other seven years of age, who sang "Our lovely
Deseret," with such earnestness that they were
highly applauded. President Brown sang a
song composed for the occasion on "Brigham
Young pioneering the Saints to the West."
Brother A. C. Brower made very instructive re-
marks to the children, and also Brother Gibbs
gave us some good advice. Brother Whitehead,
our organist, was in attendance, and we had a
general good time. "Closed with singing, "Al.
ways Cheerful." Prayer by President H. Brow-
er. H. Brower, President; M. Monson, Vice-
President; M. W. Thompson and E. Jarvis,
Counselors.				M. A. WEBB, Secretary.

	The Primary Association of East Bountiful,
held their first Annual Meeting in the Taber-
nacle, April 19th, 1880. There were present,
President Anson Call, Counselor William Thur-
good, William Muir and several more of the
brethren. Sister E. R. Snow and others were
expected but did not come, which was a great
disappointment. There were quite a number
present. We had recitations and singing from
the children, also some very good instructions
from President Call, Counselor Thurgood and
William Muir.
	The President of the Association made some
very appropriate remarks; after the close of the
meeting, there were presents distributed among
the children to the amount of $27,30, consisting
of 38 Diamond Dictionaries, two Child's Natural
Histories, twelve of George Q. Cannon's "First
Mission" and other books and cards for the
smaller children.
	About six weeks ago,some of the larger children
of the Association got up a surprise party, and
went to President Ann Dustan's and presented
her with a set of glass dishes, as a token of re-
spect; also, three pairs of dishes to the Counsel-
ors and Secretary.
	The children seem to be progressing very well.
Many of the children manifest a willingness,
when called upon, to speak or dismiss meetings.
						OLIVE C. SESSIONS.


[Column 1]
A STORM IN THE MEDITERRANEAN.

[In the very region of St. Paul's shipwreck.]

	The following is an extract from a letter re-
ceived by Dr. Ellen B. Ferguson, of this city,
from her sister, a missionary in Palestine,
in charge of the Episcopalian Orphanage at
Nazareth—

	Sunday, Sept. 21, 1879.—Mrs. G. and I had
been early to bed the previous evening, with a
view to being also early to rise, as the sailors had
told us we might expect to pass Messina about 6

[Column 2]
o'clock the next morning, and had planned such
nice readings and talks; the glorious weather we
had all the way from Marseilles having led us to
forget two important factors in our reckoning;
now if we make any plan we are careful to say
wind and weather permitting. The view when
we went on to the upper deck was magnificent.
The rocky coasts of Italy and Sicily on either
hand. I read the Litany, Psalms, etc., for the
day to Mrs. G., but there was a heavy ground
swell on, and we thought we had better find a
more comfortable seat than the one we had been
occupying. Mrs. G. went down to the cabin for
something she required, but did not return, and
by the time I had begun to wonder at her long
absence, I found it more prudent to remain
quiet myself. I had not been ill all the
way and did not wish to run any risk.
The wind increased continually and the
heavy clouds ahead of us soon burst in
sharp showers; while thunder was heard in the
distance. The storm raged fiercer; suddenly we
seemed to be enveloped in hissing, crackling
flames and roaring sound. Every one sprang
up and looked first at each other and then
round at the vessel. There seemed to be great
commotion among the sailors, a number of them
rushed to one particular spot and one whispered
to me under his breath, "la pondre." The en-
gines now stopped while the vessel was being
examined and as no injury was found anywhere
it was concluded that the electric current had
been diverted into the sea, though even the
Captian said he could not be sure it had not
passed by the conductor attached to the mast.
Before this announcement was made I went
down to the saloon to look for Mrs. G. whom I
found in her favorite corner not much alarmed,
having only heard a tremendous clap of thun-
der. I stayed with her a little while and then
went back to the upper deck. There the French
Dr. attached to the ship came and congratulated
me on my courage, which gave me an opportu-
nity of commencing a conversation with him that
lasted nearly two hours. He confessed
to being totally ignorant in matters
of religion, but promised to read anything that
I gave him. I soon found that he was a red-hot
Democrat. I wished I had had a French Bible,
but fortunately I found the Gospel of St.
Matthew and a little tract in French. I said to
the Doctor, I hoped the storm was over. "No,
madame," he replied, "it has not yet commenc-
ed." At this time the sun shining brilliantly,
the sea was a more exquisite blue than it had
been even at Naples; any ultramarine ever put
on canvass would be only a mockery of it. Soon
there followed a lurid calm, sweltering, suffocat-
ing heat, and how I longed for one breath of the
winds of God. They came indeed before long in
fitful gusts at first, but later on, in terrible fury.
The roof of the saloon forms the upper deck
which is covered in fair weather by an awning.
When the sailors came to take this in we knew
what to expect, and the steward advised us to
descend if we did not wish to be drenched. In
the saloon we found Mrs. Zunacool, a Greek
lady, and her little girl in a state of extreme
terror on account of the light which was flash-
ing incessantly all around us on the horizon. As
the Captain passed the door, madame begged
him to come in and reassure the child. He said
the weather would not be worse than it was
then, but I think I should have known from his
manner that this was false even if experience
had not taught me that sailors, like doctors,
think it their duty to suppress unpleasant truths.
As the darknes gathered the lightning became
more vivid. For some time the wind had been
shifting to all points of the compass, driving up
the clouds all around us, when we were thor-
oughly enveloped it settled behind us, and for
six hours pursued our now little shivering
vessel like a lurid, stinging flame. When I
speak of incessant lightning I mean incessant,
for it was only about every three or five minutes
that there was sufficient interval between the
flashes to show the blackness of darkness that
reigned in their absence. Sometimes the light
was mauve, sometimes greenish, producing most
magnificent effects on the white foaming waves.
Suddenly it seemed as if we were surrounded by
by batteries of artillery all concentrating their
fire upon us, and the bullets rattling and crash-
ing all around us but especially on the roof of the
saloon. It was no use asking what was the
matter, for no voice could be heard. I went to
the door and met a gentleman entering with a
hailstone in his hand, a solid cube of ice, at
least 1½ inches every way. Later on the second
officer brought in some the size of an ordinary
egg. Till now I had not seen one [--] the flashes of
lightning and it was a sight neve[-] to be forgot-


[Column 1]
ten. I can compare them to nothing but quiv-
ering cataracts of molten fire poured from the
clouds into the sea. As there was nothing to
cause reverberation the thunder was not extra-
ordinary, but we knew that every single clap
meant one of the fiery cataracts descending on
one or the other side of us. There was one gen-
tleman in a state of the most abject terror, and
when complimented by some of the other pas-
sengers on my courage I was glad to be able to
bear testimony to the power that was keep-
ing me not only safe but calm; we remained
up stairs until the saloon was closed for the
night, about 11 o'clock, but though the storm
had somewhat abated, there was no chance of
sleeping, for the fog signals were shrieking and
roaring every few minutes. Imagine if you can
with what feeling we read the psalm for the
day (107th.) "They that go down to the sea in
ships," etc. "These see the works of the Lord
and his wonders in the deep."
	How often I had pictured to myself our arrival
in the "Morning Land," and how sure I felt all
the time, that the reality would be something
quite different from any of my pictures, and so
it was.
	The Austrian Lloyd's steamer Oreste, in which
we embarked at Port Said, contrasted most em-
phatically with La Leyne of the Messageries, at
sea or at anchor, she was equally unpleasant, and
poor Mrs. Gilbert was ill from the first and all
the time. I, though not actually ill, never felt
sufficiently at ease for sketching, hardly for
reading. At Jaffa, where we touched early on
Monday 6th, a whole colony of Germans came
on board, and made a genuine picnic on the
deck, conspicuous among them a bridal pair
whom we afterwards found to be Dr. Schmidt
(British Consut at Halifax) and his bride, re-
turning from the wedding. Both then and
since Dr. Schmidt has been extremely kind and
useful, especially in getting my packing cases
through the custom house. I wish I could give
you any idea of the scene at the moment
when the invading army of Arabs
that swarm around a vessel arriving in
port are permitted to swoop upon the prey, the
unlucky passengers. They come round about you
like bees, not being allowed in the saloon or the
cabins, they stand at the door craning their long
brown necks inside, and jabber and yell and
stretch out their long lean arms and clutch at
you with their skinny fingers. How thankful
we were on arriving at Haifa about sunset to
learn from Yusuf Souidan, the agent, who came
on board, that Mr. Bellamy and Mr. Huber were
waiting for us on the shore, and to be put in
charge of Mr. Duck, a German gentleman, so
that we were not left at the mercy of those
frantic Arabs.
	It is their pleasing practice when they have you
safe in the small boat and half way between the
steamer and the land to cease from rowing and
refuse to proceed till you give or promise an ex-
orbitant "backsheesh." Poor Mrs. Gilbert, who
had left the steamer in a partially limp and bat-
tered condition, was now almost fainting and
would heve been an easy prey, but Mr. Duck of
course would not stand any nonsense. He was
telling me that we could only reach the land by
being carried on the back of an Arab, when we
found ourselves aground in the surf, and again
surrounded by swarms of yelling Arabs. I
was trying to explain the situation to
Mrs. G. when we saw Mr. Duck go over the side
and had only time to remark that his figure as
he disappeared in the darkness was not digni-
fied, when we also were seized by long brown
arms, and invited to "Get on my back Missus."
So over we went, and I had not thought much
about anything but keeping my toes out of the
water, when I was deposited on terra firma,
and a cheery voice enquired out of the darkness,
"Is that Mrs. Gilbert or Miss Brook?" Having
proclaimed my identity, I was most warmly
welcomed by Mr. Bellamy and then by Mr.
Huber, and then we turned to look, or rather
feel for Mrs. Gilbert, and soon we were jolting
off to the hotel of the German Colony in a wagon
of primitive construction. How thankful we
were to sink down, at last, in a fresh, clean bed,
and how little we cared that the floor of our
room was of pebbles and flints, and the appoint-
ments of the plainest and roughest. Everything
that night was German, and not till the morn-
ing did we realize the "Morning Land," when
we looked out on Carmel, with its monastery,
and the sweep of the Bay of Aere, with its white
houses and fringe of palm trees on the blue Me-
diterranean, with its white winged vessels gleam-
ing in the radiant sunshine, and over all the
dazzling Syrian blue. Mrs. G. made a sketch of

[Column 2]
it all, after breakfast while I was finish-
ing my letters for the mail. We intended
to start at 10 a.m. but did not get fairly off till
an hour later, a delay which we had reason to
regret at the end of the journey. The one ve-
hicle of Haifa, which has any features of a car-
riage, had been secured for us and the wagon
for our luggage. Our mounted escort consisted
of Mr. Huber, Mr. Bellany and the Houri
Jerins, the native C. M. S. clergyman from
Mujadel who had ridden in some fifteen miles to
greet us. The one decent road in the country is
that made by the German's of the colony from
Haifa to Nazareth but one does not duly esti-
mate its superiority till after trying a few of
the other roads. To us it seemed more like a
track through an illimitable stone quarry than a
road. Quitting the shores of the Great Sea we
found the region increasingly wild, stony and
barren. The Kishon at this time of the year is
merely a trickling stream and after crossing it
we went on for miles over the sandy, stony,
burning plain, passing here and there a few
goats or oxen grazing on the bare sand as it
seemed, for we could not perceive a blade of
grass anywhere. Not a cloud in all the
expanse of blue, and the sun shining
in his strength. With what force the
words of the prophet came to our minds, "The
shadows of a great rock in a weary land" as we
looked at the deep blue shadow away on the
sides of the Carmel range. We did not feel the
heat as much as we should have done but for
our experience in Cairo and the Desert.
	After about two hours on the plain we reached
the oak forest, which was very disappointing.
The Arabs from far and near have all these cen-
turies been cutting at it for firewood, and as the
idea of planting young trees to take the place of
the old ones has never entered their heads, it is
not surprising that it did not come up to our idea
of a forest. Under the half way oak, one of the
largest of the trees, we dismounted, the horses
were unharnessed and taken into the shade with
their nose bags, and Mr. Huber refreshed us
with bread and cheese, grapes and impromptu
lemonade. Here it was that a young English
gentleman named Gale, who had rashly set out
to walk to Haifa by himself, was murdered
about two years ago, and his remains only found
when search was made on his being missed, a
fortnight later. "Here is my friend" he said,
when taking leave of Miss Tanner; show-
ing a splendid revolver, and for the sake
of this "friend," it is believed that
he was killed, stunned first by a blow from
behind while he was resting under the tree.
	When we were again en route, the Houri rode
forward to prepare his wife for our approach, and
she, having first ordered her house for visitors,
mounted her husband's horse and came out
about half a mile to meet and welcome us.
Leaving the horse with an attendant, she
mounted to the seat between us on the carriage
and kissed us on both cheeks. It was a some-
what embarassing interview, as after the first
exchange of courtesies, our intercourse was
limited to smiles and nods, and I was glad when
we alighted at Mujadel. The place and our recep-
tion there I must describe in full

THE ORPHANAGE.

	Nazareth, Oct. 27, Thermometer, in my room
75, in the sun 100.
	The illustration in Farrer's "Life of Christ,"
"Nazareth from the southwest," gives a good
idea of the situation of this fine building which
is often called the palace and such it is by com-
parison with anything to be seen in this part of
the Holy Land. From the delightful balcony,
on to which the large hall opens, we have a
view which I imagine is hardly to be equalled,
and we often watch a few moments at sunrise,
sunset, moon or starlight as well as during the
"all golden" days and feast upon it. There lies
the town with its flat white roofs surrounded by
its amphitheatre of hills. Beyond the hills south-
ward stretches the plain of Esdradon, bounded
by the hills of Samaria, and eastward a succes-
sion of hills, then the Jordan Valley, and then
again the hills of Gilead and Moab distinctly
visible through the "diaphanous air." It is all
lovely even now when the slopes are parched
and brown, what would it be in early spring?
Come back to the house and let me show you
the large lofty apartments, the immense hall
in the centre of the building intended as a din-
ing hall, when the funds shall allow of the
place being filled, the dormitories with their
long rows of little white beds. On the other
our bed-rooms and sitting-rooms, all with mar-
ble floors and all spotlessly clean; cleanliness

[Column 3]
and order are the Lares and Penates of the in-
stitution and a grand lesson it is for these Arabs
whose life is passed in indescribable dirt and
disorder, to show them over the place. There
is room for 100 girls but only beds for 35. If any
one would like to do a real kindness let them
them send us £12 for a bed a year for three
years and we shall be able to take in one of the
fifty poor little creatures for whom application
has been made, or if they cannot do so much the
£2 for an additional bed. It is a sight for "sair cen"
to see the procession of 36 brownies in their
pink print frocks and white muslin veils wend-
ing down the narrow, steep, dirty, stony streets
of Nazareth on their way to church, and a sad
contrast to look at the swarms of grinning, rag-
ged little street Arabs who stand back against
the walls to watch them pass. These 36 girls,
ranging from 4 to 16 years of age, are taught to
read and write and are made thoroughly ac-
quainted with the Scriptures. Those who have
sufficient ability are taught English and trained
as teachers. There are only two servants.

To be Continued.


WOMAN'S EXPONENT.


CORRESPONDENCE

Dear Editor:
	On Thursday, June 3d, we started on a tour
through Southern Utah. As steam carried us
swiftly along, the soul felt to exclaim, in the
language of the great Morse, "What hath God
wrought." The long and weary miles that were
once traveled so tediously, are now skimmed
over as lightly and pleasantly as the little bird
flits through the air.
	To a lover of the beautiful, how grandly sub-
lime are these dear old mountains. As the eye
feasted upon the beauteous panorama of hill and
dale and snow-capped peak, and e'en the wide
expanse of blue above, that methinks Italian
skies could not excel—I thanked God for living
in a land so lavishly dowered with nature's
charms. Passing around the mountain's point,
where in early days we feared and trembled
with the pitching to and fro of the old wagon, we
beheld the placid waters of Utah Lake, still ever
the same and unchanged, on whose shores our
childish feet had made imprints in the shining,
shifting sands, and our nimble fingers gathered
the romantic shells. Passing still on and glanc-
ing to the left, what memories flooded the heart at
sight of the hills that rose before us like old fa-
miliar friends, not a hill we have not climbed,
not a crag nor a cliff we had not scaled, nor
a dale we left unscanned for the dainty violet,
the wild honey suckle, and the sweetest and
fairest of flowers—the Lily of the Valley. To-
day other feet tread the same paths, other hands
cull the lovely flowers, while we, thronged
with life’s cares and burdens, scarce have
time for pleasant retrospection.
	How soon do we pass from the free and joyous
days of youth—those days fraught with happi-
ness and bright anticipation to the cold, stern and
sometimes withering realities of life. But even
though experience teaches us that life is a pro-
bation, wherein we must learn to endure, over-
come, and suffer and make ourselves perfect
through suffering, we still cling to it tenaciously.
	At 10 a. m. we reached Provo, stepping from
from the cars we soon found a conveyance which
took us to the door of our esteemed friend, "Ma
Smoot," who although a comparative stranger,
greeted us kindly. Where on earth do we find
that sympathy and cordiality on meeting each
other for the first time that we do among Saints.
We feel a kindred tie that binds the heart at
once. She was just off for fast meeting, so we
accompanied her. And felt well, as we ever do,
in listening to inspired teaching.
	In the afternoon the Relief Societies of the
four wards met in their respective School-houses.
I attended that of the 1st Ward, while “Ma
Smoot” and Father Graves (the friend of wo-
man) drove around to the different wards to no-
tify them of our lecture in the evening. The
word was well circulated, consequently a good
audience. At the close of this public lecture to
both ladies and gentlemen, I announced my in-
tention of forming a class for a series of private
lectures for the ladies. The idea met with ap-
proval, and quite a number accordingly met
me on Friday afternoon at the B. Y. Academy,
several of the students of this institution joining
the class.	
	Saturday afternoon attended an entertainment
given by the Primary Association of the 1st
Ward. The exercises were very credit-
able, both to the children and their
President. At the close a rich repast


was served to all, the little folks here having the
preference. The Bishop remarked these things
are to encourage the children, let them be wait-
ed on first. We liked the idea; children should
not be made to feel they are of no importance, it
lessens their dignity and self-respect.
	At 5 p.m., gave the last lecture of the series,
which met with general satisfaction and appre-
ciation.
	A prominent physician lately remarked, "The
women of Utah are the most ignorant upon their
own physical organization of any class I have
ever met." In the past, it is true, we have not
enjoyed those facilities for this higher and very
desirable education possessed by the older east-
ern cities, endowed as they are with their hos-
pitals, collages and houses of learning, yet we
feel and know we have here talent and ability,
and all that is needed is the opportunity where-
by it may be developed. As we grow wiser
both in theory and experience we come to realize
there is nothing that will better secure useful-
ness, and as a result of that usefulness- happi-
ness- than health; and in order that we should
possess health, we must understand and prac-
tice the great physical laws that govern these
mortal beings. Feeling the great need of this
knowledge among women, I have, for some time
past been engaged in the pleasing task of teach-
ing and inculcating these truths by a series of
lectures. I feel ambitious for the women of
Utah. I would wish them to be the best, purest
and noblest, the wisest and most thoroughly
educated of any women on earth, and for their
emancipation from ignorance and tradition I am
willing to work earnestly and energetically.
	Ever feeling interested in woman's cause,
which you, with voice and pen, so nobly advo-
cate, I will say adieu for the present.
					E. R. SHIPP, M. D.


16		WOMAN'S EXPONENT.


PRONUNCIATION OF ENGLISH NAMES

	A recent work published by F. Warne & Co.,
London, entitled, "The Manners and Tone of 
Good Society," has a chapter on the pronuncia-
tion of the proper names and titles of the Eng-
lish aristocracy, which will be of peculiar service
to those who are much given to the reading of
of English history or English novels. The fol-
lowing condensation of the examples furnished
by the author are given herewith—Such are
Cholmondeley, pronounced Chumley; Majori-
banks, pronounced Marshbanks; Cockburn, pro-
nounced Coburn; and Cowper, pronounced Coo-
per, Again, Mainwaring is Mannering; McLeod
is McCloud. In Elgin and Gillott the "g" is
hard; in Gifford and Nigel it is soft; in John-
stone the "t" should not be sounded. In Moly-
neux the "x" is sounded, and the name is pro-
nounced Molynoox, with a very slight accent on
the last syllable. In Vaux the "x" is also sound-
ed, but it is mute in Des Vaux and likewise in
Devereux. In Ker, Berkeley, and Derby, the
"e" has the sound of "a" in far. In Waldegrave,
the second syllable, "de," should be dropped.
and so should th&nbsp;"th" in Blyth. Dillwyn is
Dillun, and Lyveden—Livden. In Conyngham,
Monson, Monkton and Ponsonby, the "o" takes
the sound of "u," and Blount should be
pronounced as Blunt, the "o" being
mute. Buchan should be pronounced Buckan,
and Beauclerk or Beauclerc is Boclare, the ac-
cent being on the first syllable, Wemyses
should be pronounced Weims, and D'Eresby—
D'Ersby. In Montgomerie the "t" is elided and
the two "p's" have the the sound of "u" the ac-
cent being on the second syllable. In Hertford
the "t" is elided and the "e" has the sound of
"a" in far. Strachan should be pronounced
Strawn; Colquehoun is Koohoon, the accent be-
ing on the last syllable; Beauchamp is Beacham,
and Coutts is Koots. Another formidable name
to the uninitiated is Duchesne, which should 
be pronounced Dukarn; Bethune should be
Beeton; and in Abergavenny the "av" is not
sounded. Menzies is pronounced Mynges; Knol-
lys as Knowls; Sandys as Sands; Gower as Gorr,
Milnes as Mills. Finally, Dalziel should be pro-
nounced Deeall, with the accent on the first
syllable; Chartres is Charters; Glamis is Glarms;
Geoghegan should be pronounced Gaygan, and
Ruthven is Riven. We may add that the ac-
cent is frequently misplaced in pronouncing
British proper names, and a few of the words
liable to mistake may be here noted. In Tadema
and Millais, the accent is on the first syllable:
in Clanricarde and Breadalbane, on the second;
while in Burnett, Bardett, Kennaird, Parnell
and Tremayne, the last syllable is accented.—
Transcript.


FAULT FINDING

		     BY JULIA SPENCER

	This is one of the greatest of evils. One begins
before they are aware saying something about
their neighbors, or some unfortunate person,
that has strayed from the path of duty, or who
has taken some wrong step that excites the pub-
lic mind. We are all apt to take such a subject
for our topic to talk about at some bee, or social
gathering, and thus give vent to what we think,
meaning no harm, and little thinking how much
harm will be the result. One commences and 
tells what she knows or has heard; it soon
spreads through the crowd. Each has some
story, or a fault to add,so they get the faults and
wrongs picked out of a person's character, and
portray them in such glaring colors, that if a
stranger to the said person happened to be pres-
ent, he would readily come to the conclusion
that that person had never done a good deed in
all his life; for truly, I have never heard of .a
person, he or she, who was past forgiveness.
When faults, mistakes, wrongs, and evil deeds
are all put in one heap, some truth and some
fiction, and then spread before our eyes in the
blackest dyes, no good can we discern.
	Now, my dear sisters, stop! How wonld we
like our faults arrayed in such a manner? for
none can deny having them, for none of us are
perfect. Supposing we take the good deeds of
the person for our topic and portray them in just
as bright colors as we have the faults, and when
we have finished, O, what a contrast! The
good outshines the evil, so that it lessens the
magnitude of faults until it looks like a speck in
the ocean, The view of this person’s character
is greatly changed. If we would take this course,
we would see more beauty in human nature
and make a better record for ourselve; and we
would be a more happy and peaceable commu-
nity.


[Column 1]
WARNER'S
SAFE
BITTERS
	It is the best Blood Purifier, and stimulates
every function to more healthful action, and is
thus a benefit in all diseases.

WOMAN'S EXPONENT.

The Rights of the Women of Zion, and the Rights of the Women of all Nations.

VOL. 9.		SALT LAKE CITY, UTAH, JULY 1, 1880.		No. 3.


OFFENCES VERSUS TRUTH.

	"Thy Word is Truth."—Jesus.
	"It must needs be that offenses come but wo, to him
by whom they come"

While facts are true, we needs must rue—
	That oft they are offensive;
But Truth—the Eternal word of God—
	Is pure, as comprehensive.
His Truth is pure, it must endure,
	For Right it sanctions ever:
His law we deem the Truth supreme—
	'Tis just, and wrongs us never.

Too much alas! for Truth doth pass,
	Man's soul it oft embitters;
The good inclined, are greived to find,
	"All is not gold that glitters."
Perchance we trust, to men unjust,
	And find them much too clever;
And so, forsooth, we blame the Truth,
	But Truth betrays us never.

And not a few, whose hearts are true—
	Whose simple word suffices,
By sharpers bold are caught and sold,
	Betrayed by mean devices.
Pretentions and pretenders too,
	Should be combated ever;
Fraud, should be sifted through and through,
	But Truth, defrauds us never.

'Tis well, 'tis wise, to scrutinize
	Their plans who feign to bless us;
'Tis braver far, with wrong to war
	Than to aid what would oppress us.
Truth! S[-]ck and hold as purest gold,
	That men from dross dissever,
Is aught amiss? be sure of this,
	By Truth, we're injured, never.

Why should we choose, to Truth abuse,
	To good by evil measure?
	If others lie, shall we deny
The Truth? In our displeasure?
	Truth makes us free, and yet we see—
	The wheat and tares together;
Alas! these tares, exist as snares—
	But Truth, ensnares us never.

These tares must with the wheat remain
	Until the harvest season;
Why 'tis ordained, Christ has explained,
	And potent is the reason.
When God doth reap, no tares He'll keep,
	They'll all be burnt together;
But the garner'd wheat, all clean and sweet
	Like Truth, will perish, Never!

Lo! God is pledged to wisdom give
	Most liberally and surely
To every one, upbraiding none,
	Who ask Him for it purely.
Armed with this key, both you and me
	Offences can out-weather;
'Tis wisdom's plea—the just will be
	By Truth offended, Never!

What-ever others say or do,
	Or friend, or foe, or brother,
We must be to our conscience true—
	We're judged not for another.
For strength each day, 'tis well to pray,
	To help our true endeavour
Offences lead the weak astray,
	But truth misleads us never.

					EMILY HILL WOODSMANSEE.


THE ladies engaged in home industries and
enterprises of various kinds, should make a good
representation of their several departments of
work on the 24th inst, especially as they enjoy
the privilege of the franchice, of which nearly
all the women are deprived.


[Column 1]
BYRON'S MONUMENT.

BY HANNAH T. KING.

	The mortal remains of England's greatest
poet, have been in the tomb fifty-five years,
with no other monument than the simple tab-
let placed in Hucknal Church, in the vaults of
which lie his mother and many of his ancestors,
and where his remains repose by the side of his
mother, by his own request. This tablet is Gre-
cian of pure, white marble, and bears the follow-
ing inscription in Roman capitals.
	In the vault beneath Carebury, where are
many of the ancestors of his mother, lie the
remains of George Gordon Noel Byron, Lord
Byron of Rochdale, in the County of Lancaster,
the author of "Childe Harold's Pilgrimage."
	This, with a circular medallion upon the
House in Holles Street, Cavendish Square, Lon-
don, where he was born, have hitherto been
the only public memorials of one, whose rest-
ing place was legally and honorably won in the
great Mausoleum of the country, Westminster
Abbey; but through the puritanical, canting,
small-mindedness of Doctor Blomfield, Bi-
shop of London; at that time, the doors were
shut against the illustrious dead! The subject
was brought before the House of Lords, and the
great Lord Brougham defended the case, and an
angry altercation between him and Bishop
Blomfield took place in that august assembly,
but the Bishop carried the day, and Huckmal
Church, like a fostering nurse, opened her doors
and received the precious remains. Could By-
ron have dipped his magical pen into the
ink that he ever made immortal, would
not the scene have been a second edition of
"English Bards and Scotch Reviewers?"
	Five years have elapsed since, at a meeting
of the enthusiastic promoters of a memorial to
Lord Byron, held at Willis's Rooms, under the
presidency of Lord Beaconsfield, a stanza from
the fourth canto of "Childe Harold" was quoted
with telling force by one noble speaker. Re-
minding his appreciative hearers that the
famous poet was not without consciousness of
his own assured immortality, Lord Rosslyn re-
peated the lines:

"But I have lived, and have not lived in vain;
My mind may lose its force, my blood its fire,
And my frame perish e'en in conquering pain;
But there is that within me which shall tire
Torture and Time, and breathe when I expire;
Something unearthly, which they deem not of,
Like the remembered tone of a mute lyre,
Shall on their soften'd spirits sink, and move
In hearts all rocky now the late remorse of love."

	Lord Byron penned these lines from the
"Eternallity," was there not a prophetic fore-
taste in his mind, that seven years after his
death the greatest of English reviewers would
speak of him in his old enemy the Edinburgh
Review, in the following terms: "At 24 Lord
Byron found himself on the highest pinnacle of
literary fame with Scott, Moore, Wordsworth,
Southey, and a crowd of other distinguished
writers beneath his feet! There is scarcely an
instance in history of so sudden a rise to so dizzy
an eminence." From 1831 to the present time
the fame of the noble poet has suffered neither
abatement or decline. Despite the insurrection
which has now and again burst forth against
some of his dark portrayals, the reaction of public
opinion, not only in England, but especially in
the United States, and wherever the English
tongue is spoken, have set "the most celebrated
Englishmen of the 19th century" upon a sum-
mit to which eyes and hearts of all countries are
offering their incense of love and gratitude!
	But to return to the doings of this committee
convened as before described. Once again was
an effort made to render something like the just
homage due from a great country to one of her
greatest sons, and at last to these promoters of
Byron's monument England is indebted for
the bronze statue, by Belt, now placed in Ham-
ilton Gardens. By the efforts of this committee,
three thousand five hundred guineas were col-
lected from the pockets of men and women scat-
tered far and wide over the earth, who, in Em-

[Column 2]
erson's words, "speak the commercial and con-
quering tongue of England." At last the poet
is before his country, the land of his birth; as he
looked and seemed when in 1813, he was the idol
of London society, the cynosure of every eye.
Mr. Belt is the favored artist, and his work will
render him an immortality in art. The statue
is in a sitting posture in deep repose, and the
manuscript of "Childe Harold" upon his knee;
contemplating from the rock that supports his
colossal figure, the ocean, which was ever his de-
light. At his feet crouches the Newfoundland
dog "Boatsawin," whose epitaph his noble mas-
ter wrote so feelingly for his love and constancy.
The yachting dress in which the figure is
clothed, suggests the element upon which the
poet's eyes are bent, and the entire absence of
affectation in his mien and attidude harmonizes
with his well-known antipathy to the theatrical
and pretentious ways. The generous Greek Gov-
ernment have contributed the pedestal, which
is of Hellenic marble, hewn from the quarries of
the Peloponnes, in honor of the hero, who, "hav-
ing dreamed that Greece might still be free,"
died in his efforts to restore her to liberty!
	In presence of this statue, let the voice of
blame, which may have some justice in it, and
of censure, which is wholly malignant, be for
ever silenced, as, in common with Sir Walter
Scott, writing upon the day following Lord By-
ron's death, "We feel as if a great luminary of
heaven had suddenly disappeared from the sky,
at the moment when every puny telescope was
levelled for an examination of the spots which
had dimmed its brightness." According to Lock-
hart, Scott's poem of "Rokeby" had failed alto-
gether to gain public approbation, because
simultaneously with its appearance "the deeper
and darker passions of 'Childe Harold' the auda-
city of its morbid voluptuousness, and the
melancholy majesty of the numbers in which it
defied the world, had taken the general imagi-
nation by storm." Such, however, was the
generous verdict of the author of "Rokeby"
which "Childe Harold" had smothered.
	Hucknal Church is about two miles from
Byron's paternal home, Newstead Abbey, and
about the same distance from Annesley, the
home of Mary Chaworth. In these regions the
poet boy had written his first effusions, and con-
ceived the most impassioned and perhaps the
purest love of his life for Mary Chaworth. I
would like to write more, but have no space but
for, Resquiescat in pace.


NOTES AND NEWS.

	Mrs Garfield is reported to have been called
upon by more than 2000 people at Mentor,
June 8th. The lawn of her pretty home was
crowded by eager talkers, and in the drawing-
room stood Mrs. Garfield receiving the congra-
tulations other visitors with great composure,
yet with signs of frank pleasure.—Woman's Jour-
nal.

	It is always healthy for young people also and
for everybody to note the rise of a man like Gar-
field, and of a woman, we may add, like Mrs.
Garfield. It is an old tradition, but it is a prec-
ious one that comes true every state and national
election, that future presidents, governors and
statesmen and their wives sit on the benches of
the primary schools of to-day,and taste the life of
infancy in all the common-place circumstances of
the great people.

	Mrs Garfield will honor the White House by
her gentle presence. She is a woman of a sweet
and winning spirit, and of bright and cultivated
mind. As Miss Lucretia Rudolph she was an
exceedingly pretty and interesting girl, and her
marriage with the General was on both sides one
of ideal affection. Her intellect has kept pace
with her husband’s; she has studied the book she
studied, taken up languages with him, and has
so trained herself as to fit her boys for college in
the most thorough manner. She is an excellent
Latin scholar, and is also proficient in several
modern languages. She is a little lady, graceful
in carriage,and having most frank and charming
manners.


WOMAN'S EXPONENT.


[Column 1]
		LIFE INCIDENTS

	BY HELEN MAR WHITNEY.

	The scenes which are familiar to the Latter-
day Saints of an earlier day, are but little known
to those who have since been gathered from the 
different nations, nor to those who have grown
up in the peaceful valleys of the Rocky Moun-
tains. Until within a few years, the world
knew nothing of our true history. Falsehoods
were manufactured and sent out to serve the
purposes of our enemies, and apostates have been
their willing tools. The bitter prejudices felt by
the outsido world makes it almost an impossi-
bility for them to believe or become acquainted
with our faith and principles; but the history
ot this people is always interesting to the Latter-
day Saints, and incidents of our travels and ex-
perience are calculated to benefit the young and
rising generation. The spirit of unbelief which
has crept into our midst is lamentable, and a
stupor seems to have come over a portion of this
people, who, I sometimes fear, will need the
judgments of God to awaken them from its influ-
ence, and we know that they are sure to come
upon the slothful and disobedient, if they do not
repent.
	I can truly say that I feel an interest in the
welfare of all, and if some of the incidents of my
life could impress the minds of others, as they
have my own, I would feel amply repaid for
writing them. There seems to be a great curi-
osity in the minds of strangers about the “Mor-
mon” women, and I am willing, nay, anxious,
that they should know the true history of the
faithful women of Mormondom. In the brief
sketches which have been given from time to
time, the trials and sufferings of the Latter-day
Saints have scarcely been touched upon. Since
writing my reminiscences I have thought of the
names of many who lived and died for the truth.
Among these was the widow of Brother David
W. Patten. She was a noble and self-sacrificing
woman, who left all for the Gospel’s sake, and
her husband being a missionary, she was
early thrown upon her own resources, and
though she had a slight and delicate frame she
had a persevering and energetic spirit, was
neat, and naturally of a refined nature and
could not be happy in idleness. She was a
seamstress by trade and worked for her living.
The hardships and privations incident to a
Western life, particularly to the Latter-day
Saints, soon broke her down and brought on
consumption. After her husband was killed,
she, being, like the rest destitute, felt that she
must do something for her support, and not
finding anything else that she could do, conclud-
ed to take a few boarders. Among them was a
young man who, though not a member of our
Church, bore a good character, and, to be brief,
he loved her, and seeing her lonely condition,
proposed to marry her. She accepted. This
step, at the time, caused many to think her
weak in the faith. When we afterwards met
her in Quincy,Illinois, she told my parents why
she married without asking counsel, said she
was no longer able to work and had no one to
take care of her, and she knew what the counsel
would be if she asked it, and not wishing to dis-
obey, she did iton her own responsibility. As
soon as he heard and-understood the Gospel he
received it. After father came, to us in Qniney,
they having a house with two rooms, gave us
one to live in while father went up to Com-
merce to prepare a place for us. His name was
Bentley, he was a carpenter by trade, industri-
ous and well able to provide everything she
needed, or desired, and though a number of
years younger than she, he was perfectly devot-
ed to her, and his study by day and by night
was to make her comfortable. No one could
show greater love and tenderness toward a wife
than he did, until her spirit took itsflightwhich,
if I remember rightly, was the second year after
the Saints settled at Nauvoo. He lived but a
few years after her death. She was an exem-
plary woman, and enjoyed the love and respect
of all who knew her. While we were living
with them in Quincy, when I was about ten
years of age, I had a severe attack of fever, and
a heavy storm coming on, the weather was
quite cold, and not having a fire-place in the
room, mother pieced a kettle of live coals in the
centre of the apartment. Just as she stepped
out into the other room, my little brother, aged
about four years, came in and accidentally fell
into the kettle, and burned him badly. I was so
frightened that I was upon the point of leaping
out of bed when mother heard him scream, but
he sprang out before she got into the room and
cried for her to anoint him with the, conse-

[Column 2]
crated oil. She immediately administered it,
and was silently praying, when he cried, “Pay
loud.” She obeyed him, and in a few minutes
he was sound asleep.  He never cried from the
burn after the oil was administered, and it was
healed from that moment. What a pity we
cannot always have faith like a little child, and
instead of calling upon doctors who have no
faith in the ordinances, call on the Great Physi-
cian, who giveth freely to all and upbraideth
not. When we can do this, there will be less
suffering and fewer graves to weep over.
	In the month of July, father moved us up to
Commerce; he pulled down an old log stable be-
longing to a Rro. Bozier, about one mile from
the river, and laid up the logs at the end of the
Bozier house (which had a number of rooms and
contained several families; he put on a few
shakes” to cover it, but it had no floor or chink-
ing; when it rained the water stood near ankle
deep on the ground; the chimney of the other
house, being built on the outside, served us as a
fireplace. My mother, not liking the dirt floor,
had a few little boards laid down to serve as a
substitute. I remember the evening of the 23d
of August, 1839,we were visited by a heavy rain
storm, and those boards floated on the water;
My mother had bread light and ready to bake in
a tin oven or reflector, and it had to be propped
up so as to bake the bread before the fire, which
was built upon andirons. Under these
peculiar circumstances I was allowed to go and
stop with one of our neighbors, and when
I returned in the morning I was informed that
a little stranger had arrived that night. This
was truly a wonderful event and created quite a
sensation in our midst. He was named after
David Patten, and although born in a stable, he
was a prince in our estimation.  This was their
sixth child, four of whom were then living.
Father purchased five acres of woodland from
Hyrum Kimball, and Brother Parley P. Pratt
purchased the same number of acres adjoining.
They went to work and cut logs and invited a
few of the old citizens; viz.: Brother Bozier Squire
Wells, Louis Robinson and others, to assist in
putting up their houses, as our people were
mostly prostrated by sickness. Brother Pratt
soon sold out his improvements and went with
his family on a mission to England. Father was
building his chimney and had just got to the ridge
of the house when he was taken down with
chills arid fever. The hardships and exposures
consequent on beidg driven from Missouri in the
winter, had made the Saints easy subjects for
the ague to prey upou in that swampy country;
nearly all were taken down, one after another,
and the ones who were not shaking or delirious
with fever, would do their best towards waiting
upon those that were. Many had to see their
dear ones die and not one of the family able to
follow them to their last resting place; hundreds
were lying sick in tents and wagons. The Pro-
phet visited and administered words of consola-
tion and often made tea and waited upon them
himself and sent members of his own family
who were able to go, to nurse and comfort the
sick and sorrowful. He was often heard to say
that the Saints who died in consequence of the
persecutions, were as much martyrs as the ones
who were killed in defence of the Saints or mur-
dered at Haun's Mill. There are many living
martyrs who remember those days and some
will yet wear a martyr’s crown. The powers of
darkness seemed to have combined to put a stop
to the work of the Almighty, but Satan’s plans
have always been frustrated, and they always
will be.
	One night while we were living in the Bozier
House, we were awakened by our mother, who
was struggling as though nearly choked to death.
Father asked her what was the matter, when
she could speak, she replied, that she dreamt
that a personage came and seized her by the
throat and was choking her. He lit a candle
and saw that her eyes were sunken and her nose
pinched in, as though she were in the last-stage
of cholera. He laid his hands upon her head
and rebuked the spirit in the name of Jesus, and
by the power of the holy Priesthood commanded
it to depart. In a moment afterwards, some
half a dozen children in other parts of the house
were heard crying, as if in great distress; the
cattle began to bellow and low, the horses to
neigh and whinnow, the dogs barked, hogs
squealed, and the fowls and everything around
were in great commotion, and in a few minutes
my father was called to lay hands on Sister
Bently,the widow of David Patten, who lived in
the next room. She was seized in a similar man-
ner to my mother. They continued quite feeble
for seyeral days from the shock.

[Column 2]
	One day after this circumstance had taken
place, my father was visiting the Prophet. In
his diary he says: “He took me a walk by the
river side and requested me to relate the occur-
rence at the Bozier house. I did so; and also told
him the vision of evil spirits in England on the
opening of the Gospel to that people. After I
had done this, I asked what all these things
meant and whether or no there was anything
wrong in me. “No, Brother Heber; at that
time when you were in England, you was then
nigh unto the Lord. There was only a vail be-
tween you and Him, but you could not see Him.
When I heard it, it gave me great joy, for I then
knew that the work of God had taken root in the
land; it was this that caused the Devil to make
a struggle to kill you.” Joseph then said, the
nearer a person approaches the Lord, a greater
power would be manifest by the Devil to pre-
vent the accomplishment of the purposes of God.
He then gave me a relation of many contests
that he had had with Satan, and his power had
been made manifest from time to time since the
commencement of bringing forth the Book of
Mormon.”
	In another place he says: “I crossed the river
to Commerce with several of the Twelve, and as
I was standing by the railing of the boat, look-
ing at thebeautiful site of Nauvoo,and I remark-
ed, ‘It is a very pretty place, but not a long
abiding place for the Saints.' These remarks
reached Elder Rigdon and family, and caused
them to feel somewhat sad, as they were well
situated in a nice stone house built by Dr. Isaac
Galland, and otherwise comfortably situated.
When we met in council in Joseph’s house, the
case of 	*		*		"was brought up for in-
vestigation and disposed of in a summary man-
ner. Elder Rigdon then arose and said he had
some feelings toward Elder Kimball, saying, I
should suppose that Elder Kimball had passed
through suffering and privations, mobbings and
drivings enough to learn to prophecy good of
Israel.’ I began to expect I was going to receive
quite a chastisement from Elder Rigdon, know-
ing his peculiar temperament, I arose upon my
feet and said, ‘President Rigdon, I’ll prophecy
good concerning you, all the time, if you can get
it?’ On hearing that, Joseph had a hearty laugh
with the brethren, when Elder Rigdon yielded
the point."


IN WHAT DOES BEAUTY CONSIST.

	There are many things in nature around us
which we look upon and recognize with enthusi-
astic ecstsay as being sublimly beautiful, filling
our souls with awe,reverence yea,love,raising our
thoughts, from mortal cares to loftier themes,
and inspiring us with adoration for the Creator
of so much beauty, harmony and melody.
	The grandeur of the mountains as ‘they tower
one above the other, higher and higher until
they appear to touch the very sky, and some-
times seem to reach far away into the realms of
space—whether they are crowned with eternal
snow, or burning with the lava-fire of nature’s
own kindling must inevitably strike the beholder
as one of the most truly beautiful features which
the earth affords.
	Who could ever have watched the glory;of an
exquisitely charming sunset without feeling
that it surpassed in real beauty all the
works of art; and that extravagant as the
language might be used to express its beauty, it
was still inadequate to the subject.
	The forests,the ocean, the lakes,and the rivers,
all these are indeed truly beautiful; the flowers
in all their vast varieties of hues and shades and
forms are among the most delightfully beautiful
of Earth’s wounderful productions. We cannot
enumerate the objects of beauty of which our
earth is filled—there is no stopping place, for
whatever is perfect, cannot fail to be beautiful.
To my mind, perfection seems to be the true de-
finition of beauty, but where there is a defect
beauty is marred. Nature’s works are perfect—
they are really beautiful.
	The sentiment of beauty is a true inspiration—
it elevates and refines the mind,in whatever form
of life it is presented. One must feel its influence,
and the inspiration of its presence in both ani-
mate and inaniamte nature.			M.
	Salt Lake City, June 1872.
