SALT LAKE CITY, JULY 15, 1880.


[Column 1]
	PLEASURE EXCURSION TO BLACK
	ROCK

	A very pleasant party of old people, almost all
over the allotted three score years and ten.
This company under the management of one of 
the most efficient and courteous committees
that could possibly be formed, went out to Black
Rock (via) the Utah Western Railway to spend
the day in social converse etc., on Thursday
July 8th, inst. They were gathered from all the 
various parts of the city, and the local regions
roundabout, in carriages, wagons and street cars.
They were accompanied by Pres. John Taylor,
Apostle Wilford Woodruff our aged veteran sisters
Elizabeth Ann Whitney and Eliza R. Snow
Smith, some other invited guests; a few news-
paper reporters and some musicians were also
members of the party.
	The train left the Depot in this city at 8. 20,A.
M. There were in addition to all the passenger
cars that could be obtained eight flat cars all
crowded.
	The ride to Black Rock was agreeable in every
respect, nothing occured to mar the enjoyment
of any, and the weather was most propitious.
Arriving at the destination about half past nine.
A star spangled banner was streaming from a
high point of the Mt., just by the Grantsville
brass band under the leadership of Bro., James
Ratcliffe and in handsome brand new uniform,
greeted the party with excellent music; Pres.
F. M. Lyman and his assistants had prepared a
bowery, tables and every convenience needful to
make the old people comfortable with lumber,
wagon cover and materials from Tooele. Re-
freshments of every kind imaginable had been
brought and were soon served,buckets of lemon-
ade were prepared and all who wished were
furnished with hot water for tea. The 7th,ward
band led by Bro. Wm. Foster played some lively
music and some of the old people were invited to
dance. Father Colborn one of Zion's Camp
aged 79,danced a hornpipe like a young boy; also
Elder Samuel Neslen 73 of S. L. City, Amos
Mc Bride Grantsville 84, and Wm. Jenkinson
16th, Ward S. L. City 72,(blind.) Sister Eastham
of Grantsville and Scotch Ann of 15th Ward S.
L. City. The company enjoyed the oocasion in
meeting with old friends and in bathing in the 
calm placid and exhilarating waters of the cele-
brated Salt Lake. A number of old people from
Tooele Co.,came to meet those from the city,and
participate in the social festivities.
	At one o'clock P. M., exercises were com-
menced in the bowery. Prayer was offered by
Elder Chapman, Wm. Foster sang "The Merry
Mormons," all joining in the chorus. Presiding
Bishop Edward Hunter; Chairman of the Ex-
cursion Committee made a few fatherly and in-
terestng remarks, Bro. Hunter is now 86 years
of age and is still full of life and vigor. Bro.

[Column 2]
Beers sang "The down hill of life." Sister Levis-
age aged 93 gave a page of her experience and 
sang a verse of the hymn "Now let us rejoice in
the day of salvation," Elder Neslen sung "The
King of the Cannibal Islands" nearly all the old
people joining in the chorus. The oldest man
was called for when Wm. Wilding aged 97 came
forward. A Duet by Bro. and Sister Moss
"We've lived and loved together." This aged
couple have lived happily together about 53
years, and have had 21 children. Elder Samuel
Neslen and his wife Sister Eunice Neslen have
been wedded 51 years, and lived in perfect har-
mony during the entire period. They have had
12 children 53 grandchildren and 6 great grand-
children.
	 President Taylor at the request of the com-
mittee addressed the assembly. He blessed the
people and those who had so thoughtfully re-
membered the "old folks." He expressed his
pleasure at being present to witness so much
happiness; remarked it was proper to pluck as
many flowers as possible in the journey of life;
spoke of the blessings of the everlasting Gospel,
and that this was what had brought so many of
us together under such favorable circumstances,
for which we of all people on the face of the
earth ought to be grateful. Said: "I suppose we
have a good many seventies here to-day; well
we are looking forward to the future when we
shall meet with those who have gone before, let
us try to benefit our fellowmen and help prepare
all that we can for the Celestial kingdom; let
union be our motto, we must be united, cleave
to God and to one another, live our religion,
keep the commandments of God, and we'll
meet on the other side of Jordan, Hallelujah, as
we used to sing." Blest Bro. Goddard and
Bro. Savage and said let us all seek to bless one
another and proposed three cheers for "the old
folks at home." Bros. Savage and Goddard pro-
posed three cheers for President Taylor, which
was responded to by the multitude.
	Bro. Savage, in behalf of the people, thanked
Bros. W. W. Riter, Hooper, Jennings, Teasdel,
also Walker Bro., and Alonzo Hyde and D. J.
Taylor of Black Rock, and other merchants and
private individuals for donating liberally towards
the excursion, and the prizes for some of the
oldest of the party, and generously assisting to
make the old folks happy. He then proposed
a vote of thanks for Pres. Lyman, of Tooele,
which was heartily accorded and three loud and
enthusiastic cheers were given for the "Repub-
lic of Tooele."
	Bro. Wm. Jenkinson (blind) sang "John, the 
sailor.'[-] Prizes were distrubted to those who
sang, danced, etc. Catherine Wilson, aged 87,
of E. T. City, who pulled a handcart across the
plains, and Daniel England, of Tooele, 81, the
oldest man present, who had drawn a handcart
across the plains, had prizes awarded them,
also two aged sisters for making neckties from
native grown silk. One of the fovorite singers
of other days, Elder William C. Dun-
bar, sang "My dear old wife and I," in
his happiest manner. The congregation sang
"Praise God," and the closing benediction was
pronounced by Bro. George Goddard. There
were many old people from Tooele and Grants-
ville. Everything passed off peaceably and
pleasantly, and some dear old people with
whom we are intimately acquainted de-
clared it the happiest day of their lives.
	It is very beautiful to see such manifestations
of reverence and respect for the aged, who are
deprived of many of the recreations and enjoy-
ments the younger and middle-aged participate
in. God bless those who thus administer, bless,
comfort and bring gladness and pleasure to the
weary ones who have toiled so long in this work-
aday world, and who are now nearing the "gold-

[Column 3]
en gates" of the celestial city. Success to Bishop
Hunter and Brothers Savage and Goddard, for
their annual entertainment of the old folks.
May they never want for any good thing, and
always meet with kindness, and a hearty God
bless you from all, both young and old.


HOME AFFAIRS.

	THE Minutes of the Quarterly Conference of
the Y. L. M. I. A. of June 19th, and further re-
marks of President Taylor in the afternoon of
that day, are crowded out again.

	WE have several reports from Primary Asso-
ciations laid by for next time, also some poetry
and other articles; and we repeat again that
we must have the real and full name of those
who send articles for publication; otherwise we
cannot use them.

	THE Home Dramatic Club will appear in the
Theater on the night of the 23d and 24, of July
in the pretty, thrilling and romantic drama of
"Rosedale." Miss Keetie Haywood will person-
ate the beautiful and fascinating Lady Florence
May. Mrs. Ardelle Cummings the piquant
charming and interesting Rosa Leigh. The
principal character Elliot Grey will be performed
by Laron Commings and Matthew Lee M. D., by
O. F. Whitney. Hebe M. Wells will appear in
a new role the gentlemanly villain Col. Caven-
dish May.

	WE are pleased to announce to our readers and
the many friends of the young lady that Miss
Mattie Paul Hughes received her diploma on the
first day of July, Commencement at the Ann
Arbor University. She writes, "It was my day
of days" with two hundred and four other grad-
uates I marched triumphantly on to the great
platform, where we were presented with our
diplomas amid the cheers of the crowded Hall
and as we descended our floral gifts were handed
us. Mine was a basket of beautiful flowers rich
and rare."
	Miss Mattie goes from the University to the
home of an invalid lady who resides on the
banks of the St. Clair river: where she will re-
main until September, then go to the New
England Hospital, Boston, Mass.

	PREPARATIONS are being made for a magnifi-
cent display in the procession of the Pioneer
Celebration on the 24th inst., which will also be
a sort of Jubilee of the Fiftieth Year since the
organization of the Church. It should certainly
be a time of general rejoicing and a "day of
days" for Israel. The Decorative Committee,
with young ladies to assist them, are beautifying
the inside of the Tabernacle, by making new
baskets and wreaths of flowers and vines, mak-
ing the place more fresh and attractive, and
with the draping and other adornments it will
present a charming picture.

	ERRATA.—In July 1st number of the EXPO-
NENT, in the article entitled "Byron's Monu-
ment," some typographical errors occur which
must be corrected. The word Carebury, in the
inscription, should have been are buried. I in-
tended the inscription printed in the form in
in which it is inscribed upon the tomb. Here
it is, as it should be:

Where many of the ancestors of his Mother are buried
				Lie the remains of
		George Gordon Noel Byron, Lord Byron
		Of Rochdale in the County of Lancaster
		Author of Childo Harold's Pilgrimage.

	The other correction is Eternal City, which
reads as published, Eternallity.
						HANNAH T. KING.

	Two meetings of the sisters were held at Draper
on Tuesday the 13th inst. The Relief Society con-
vened in the morning at ten, Mrs. Harriet Burn-


ham presiding. After singing, prayer and the
reading of minutes, the meeting was addressed
by Sisters M. I. Horne, E. B. Wells and E. R.
Snow Smith. Bishop I. M. Stewart then endorsed
the remarks of the Sisters, and added a strong
testimony of the Gospel. Singing, benedic-
tion. At noon a party of the sisters were
invited to dine at Bro. Joshua Terry's; and after
partaking of a good substantial dinner, were
shown over the grounds. Brother Terry has
five acres enclosed as a garden, which is highly
cultivated. Fruit trees of almost every variety,
luxuriant grape vines, delicious raspberries of dif-
ferent colors, also strawberries that are now near-
ly all gone. We all quite enjoyed our pleasant
ramble through the grounds. At 2 p.m. a meet-
ing was held to organize a Primary Association.
There were as many present as could crowd into
the school-house. "Sister Eliza" addressed the
children on the subject of being organized and
explained to the parents and children the need
there was of spiritual training for the young, the
great lack there had been in this regard in times
past.	*	*	Mrs. Catharine Smith was
elected President, Mrs. Emma Terry and Mrs.
Elizabeth Day, Counselors; Alice Stewart, Secre-
tary; Eliza Stewart, Assistant Secretary, and
Delia Fitzgerald, Treasurer. One hundred and
six members were enrolled. Sisters' Wells and
Horne talked a little to the children, and the
meeting closed in the usual way. The children
kept excellent order and seemed much interested
in all that was said to them.


WOMAN'S EXPONENT.


[Column 1]
			OBITUARIES.

	The sistesr of the Minersville Relief Society
have been called to mourn the loss of one of
their most faithful members, the beloved wife
of our worthy Bishop, James McKnight. She
departed this life June 3d, 1880. Her complaint
was dropsy, from which she suffered six months
and two weeks. Sister Sarah McKnight was
ever faithful to the cause of truth, was a teacher
in our society for many years, always counseling
good to the members. She has endured many
afflictions, but by the exercise of faith she al-
ways possessed, she was enabled to overcome,
until her last sickness. She was a loving wife,
an affectionate mother and a true Saint of God.
She died as she had lived, in full faith of the
Gospel, with the glorious hope of coming forth in
the resurrection of the just. The deceased, Sarah
Howell McKnight, was born December 26th,
1833, in South Wales; was baptized at the age of
twelve years; emigrated with her parents to
Australia shortly after; was married to James
McKnight April 4th, 1854; started the same day
to the home of the Saints. She leaves her hus-
band and ten children, an aged mother, and a
large circle of friends to mourn her loss.
									P. A. Z.

	Died in Spring City, Utah, June, 1880, Sister
Caroline King and Lena Olsen. Sister King has
been a member of the Church of Jesus Christ of
Latter-day Saints for 49 years, has passed
through a great many of the persecutions inci-
dent to the rise of the church. She has been
faithful to the last; a kind and dutiful wife and
mother, and we now feel assured she has gone
to enjoy the Fiftieth Year's Jubilee with the just
behind the vail. Sister Olsen was one of our
beloved teachers. She was a widow when she
emigrated from Scandinavia, but she brought
her family to Zion. She has suffered most se-
verely for three years with an inward cancer,
which finally caused her death. She was sur
rounded by a large family of children, who are
left to mourn her loss, though they were glad
she could be relieved for her suffering was so

[Column 2]
intense. She has done much good in waiting on
the sick and needy in our society.
				FANNY KOFFORD,
		In behalf of the R. S. Spring City.

	DIED in Pleasant Green, at fifteen minutes to
12 o'clock, June, 28, 1880, after an illness of two
weeks, Charlotte Brook Hirst, wife of the late
deceased John Hirst. She was born March,
1818, in Slaithwaite, England, and was baptized
into the Church of Jesus Christ of Latter-day
Saints in 1853, emigrated to Utah in 1868; was
the mother of thirteen children. She leaves one
son and nine daughters, thirty-nine grandchil-
dren and many friends, who deeply mourn her
loss. She was a good, kind, faithful wife and
mother. She has always had a desire that as
soon as the Lord saw fit to take her to her hus-
band, who died just twenty-one months and
twenty-one days before her, that it would be a
happy day. In the spring of 1879, Sister Snow
and Sister Horne organized a Relief Society in
this Ward, and Sister Hirst was elected Presi-
dent, the duties of which office she has perform-
ed well. Rather than miss her meeting she
would walk four miles. She was a woman of
the purest principles and greatest integrity, and
was well respected by all who knew her; wher-
ever she went she gained the good will of all her
associates. She was liberal to the poor, faithful
and true to her trust as a hand-maiden of God,
and as a good, useful member of the Ward in
which she lived. She has passed from earth
with a full assurance of an eternal salvation with
the redeemed and sanctified in Jesus Christ.

	"I'll praise my Maker while I've breath,
	And when my voice is lost in death,
	Praise shall employ my nobler powers;
	My days of praise shall ne'er be past,
	While life and  thought and being last,
	Or immortality endures."
								VIRA.


CORRESPONDENCE.

		ANN ARBOR, MICHIGAN June 28th, 1880.
	Aunt Em: While all nature seems hushed
after a threatening thunder-storm and the stifl-
ing dust lies beneath the carriage wheels as they
are whirling past in scores to enjoy the washed
air and the cooling breeze, and the leaves are
silvery from falling water that has so recently
bathed them, I find I have just time for once
to admire nature decked in her most beautiful
attire, and comment upon the "weather." I
almost wonder if I have just awakened from a
dream and have been asleep all these months
since I last wrote.
	Really, I have scarce had time to realize
whether I had a friend or foe, whether the moon
rose or the sun set. I have been lost to every
thing but books and lectures. You will please
remember where I turned the leaf down when I
was last talking to you: I had arrived at Ann
Arbor here I met Miss Mattie Paul Hughes she
is now very busy expecting to receive her Degree
as M. D., the 1st of July, is not this welcome
news? You would think so if you had traveled
the same road through brambles and briars, al-
though the knowledge is valuable the road is
rough and thorny, She has gained the point,
and won the prize and many readers in Utah
will have the pleasure of her acquaintance and
usefulness when she feels she has what she
wants and needs and the whisperings are—come
home.
	My visit at the college—my anticipations were
fully realized and I felt satisfied with the oppor-
tunity that I saw had opened for me. I found
just what I wanted to add to what I had at-
tained in Philadelphia I made no delay but again
the second time registered my name as a medical
student and went to work. The lectures are
now closed and with them I have completed
my studies in Chemistry and received my Cer-
tificate.	*	*	*	*	*
	It is astonishing what art and science of this
the 19th, century can do. The more one learns
the more they know: there is no end; and instead
of emerging from, they are merging into the great
ocean of knowledge, and the labor began can
only be continued in another sphere.	*	*	*


Everything pertaining to the college and lec-
tures seems well done, and well applied. The
Chemical facilities I learn cannot be equaled in
the United States. I do not know how many
hundred tables they have in the Laboratory for
Physiological, Chemical and Analytical work.
	The building is large, furnished with several
wings fitted up with gas and water works, and
suitable apparatus, yet this summer they will
still add to it. The grounds called the Campus
which is an enclosure of some 100 rods square is a
lovely place for a walk, one is shielded from the
schorching sun by the trees above, and the as-
phaltum walks so nice to the feet, and the bal-
ance is carpeted with green grass. In this en-
closure is situated the great University of Mich-
igan comprising the Department of Literature,
Science and the Arts including the Schools of
Mines) . The Department of Medicine and
Surgery the Department of Law, the schools of
Pharmacy, the Homoeopathic Medical, and the
Dental College. Two large Hospitals. There is
considerable clashing between the professors of
the Regular school of Medicine and the Homoe-
pathic, this could scarcely be expected otherwise
when the two schools are at such wide differ-
ences from each other. On the whole Ann Ar-
ber as its name implies is sufficiently feminine
to make the place attractive. More Anon.
							E. S. BARNEY.


SAN FRANCISCO, June 18th, 1880.
Editor Exponent:
	There has been nothing of late of special in-
terest at present merely the local news of a large
city which does not make a lasting impression.
Local news consists of murders, suicides, acci-
dents, embezzlements and scandals, of which we
have enough to sustain us from day to day;
sometimes we have an extra like the late awful
railroad accident. Each one of you who long
for a view of the world had best remain at home,
where you are comparatively safe and where you
can never dream the half that's being done in
the great, busy world. For the lack of other
amusement, I watch the children in these nar-
row streets, where children are so numerous,
and I reason within myself why nearly all of
them turn out bad, and I have arrived at the
couclusion that it is the lack of youthful amuse-
ments of which the children are deprived. Boys
in particular; girls can amuse themselves in the
house, but boys must be out and have breath-
ing room. All, or nearly all, of the games and
pastimes are prohibited by law. They are not
allowed to fly kites because they get entangled
with the telegraph wires; no ball games, for it
breaks people's heads; no hide-and-go-seek, for
there is no place to hide; no whistles, for there
are no trees; no sleds or skates, for there is no
snow or ice; no place to make a wagon, for there
are no boards, and for my part I sympathize
with the much abused boys; there is no place for
them to play but the street. If they go into a
park, there is a man to keep them off the grass,
and if they sit on the steps of the house, some
one throws water on them.
	The great cry is, "What shall we do with our
boys?" I think that in large cities, some suita-
ble place should be provided for them to play
beside the street. If the city should hire some
wagons to let the boys have a ride for once in
their lives, or provide some suitable amusement,
it would be cheaper than boarding them at the
State's expense. If they had something to oc-
cupy their minds, they would not get into so
much mischief. The depravity among the
children here is frightful. Boys six years of age
smoking and learning different vices from older
boys. Every day we can see little boys, seven,
eight, nine and so on, of age, taken to the pri-
son. They are initiated in crime so young that
the State prison has no terrors; and some of you
mothers who are so anxious about your own lit-
tle ones should feel content and thank God that
you are so far away from such wickedness.
	Everything seems to be on the change politi-
cally, financially and domestically, and we are
all living in a suspense; something seems to
threaten us, but what, time only will reveal.
	Respectfully,					NELLIE.


THE gavel used  at  the  Chicago  convention
was made  of  wood  from  Lincoln's  home.  the
handle was a cane  grown  on  the  Mt.  Vernon
estate.  It was presented as a souvenir to  Sena-
tor Hoar to carry home.  It is  said  he  made a
"most admirable presiding  officer" doing justice
to all interested parties and  commanding  their
respect.


WOMAN'S  EXPONENT.


CURIOSITY.

	Woman's curiosity has become proverb[-]al, and
with me the question arises, what is  curiosity?
I  think it is nothing more or, less than an in-
stinctive desire for  knowledge, and I ask myself
again, is there any thing worth  man's knowing
that woman should not know?  Woman is an in-
telligent being and has her part to act in the 
drama of life  (and  no  very  unimportant  part
either) and should feel interested in all that per-
tains  to  the welfare  of  the  human  race;  then
why should she be kept in ignorance of  existing
facts, that are proper  for  man  to  know.  For
ages [-]ast it has been considered  unpopular, and
almost  disrespectful, for woman  to  know any-
thing outside of her household affairs;  but shank
God things are changing, women are  beginning
to   understand  their  rights,  to  indulge  their 
curiosity,  and  look  and  learn  for  themselves;
they are no longer satisfied to remain in igno-
rance and stupidity.
	Knowledge when rightly  used  hurts  no  one
but tends to exalt,  none  need  be  afraid  that
woman will know too much: the more she knows
the better able she  will  be  to  act  wisely;  and
with the spirit of the Lord to help her she can
more easily and better  perform  the  duties  and
labors of life.  It will make her tasks lighter  for
she will not have so many blunders to rectify so
many wrongs to make right, she will not need
to do the same things over twice or thrice when,
she knows  where,  and how  everything  should
be done and therefore is  enabled  to  avoid  much
unnecessary  labor.  Then  let  woman  as  well
as man strive  for  knowledge, and  let  none  be
ridiculed for being curious.
	Let old  fashioned  notions  be  buried  in  the
past and let women emerge  into  the  light and
bring forth  children  of  intelligence,  who  will
help revolutionize the world.
	No wonder the world is in a  deplorable condi-
tion when women, the mothers and teachers are
not to know only the few things that man thinks
proper.  It is sad to know that so much  corrup-
tion exists in the world.  And I  do not  wonder
that man's sensitiveness is disturbed  when such
gross [-]vils are exposed to the eyes  of  the ladies,
as it has been of late in the  Museum of wax fig-
ures;  but I saw nothing there more unfit for the 
ladies to see than  gentleman, in  fact  I think  if
either is excluded it should be the men.
	By  women  seeing,  and  understanding  the
causes and effects, as mothers, they may, by pro-
per teachings prevent much of the evil that now
exists in the world.  So let no more ridicule and
reproach be cast upon woman for  being  curious.
I say success to  "Women's  cu[-]osity."
	C. DOW.
	Salt Lake City, June 1880.


THE "WORD OF WISDOM."

	The Word of Wisdom, as given in the revela-
lations, includes many th[-]ngs beside the use of
tea, coffee, or tobacco. But as those are desig-
nated there, and I often find myself thinking
about the great demand for tea and coffee by the
sisters among the Latter-day Saints, and how
few of them ever refuse the cup of tea, and that
is always presented at our social gatherings in
some localities at least, I thought I would write
a few lines upon it. That it is a growing evil
amongst us is evident from the fact that many
of our young sisters require the cup of tea to help
them to perform the most ordinary duties of life,
and without it feel themselves totally unfit for
exe[--]on of any kind. And the majority of our
young mothers not only use it constantly them-
selves, but give it to their little ones, thought-
lessly no doubt, but to their injury nevertheless,
as helping them to imbibe habits calculated to
retard their capacities for future usefulness,
which every mother should always keep in mind
in the rearing of her children. How blessed
would we be could we only keep in our hearts
that first love of the Gospel so many of us expe-
rienced in those by-gone days, when we first
embraced its principles, when with light hearts
we not only left off the use of what the Lord
said was not wise, but left comfortable homes;
many of us left fathers, mothers, sisters and bro-
thers also for the Gospel's sake,how small in com-
parison is the abstaining from the use of tea and
coffee.
	If we could only be united in all our efforts
and learn to live by every word that proceeds
from the mouth of the Lord, we would then be
blessed indeed.					AUNT MARY.

	Brigham City, Arizona, June 1st, 1880.


[Column 1]
WOMAN'S CONVENTION.

	At the concluding session of the N. W. S. A.
Convention, held in Indiaanapolis, Indiana, on
the evening of May 27th, there was a very large
and intelligent audience, and the exercises were
particularly interesting.
	First on the programme was Mrs. E. T. Housh,
of Louisville, Kentucky, who read a pointed and
witty poem, entitled "Scrub up, for the Women
are Coming." Mrs. Carr, of Louisville, reported
woman's work in Kentucky. Mrs. Mary E.
Haggart, of Indiana, made an effective address
and was warmly applauded.	*	*	*	Mrs.
Belva A. Lockwood said:
	"I would not, if I could, add anything to the
eloquent arguments of the speakers who have
preceded me, but I will say that the question
that seems most important to me is which of the
great parties of this country will put in its plat-
form the plank of woman suffrage. That party,
whatever it may be, will be the party of the fu-
ture, for as sure as I stand here to-night, not a
decade will pass, before every woman iu this
country, black and white, will have the right to
vote. The mouth of woman is no longer closed,
the fear has been lifted, and we must become
citizens. It has been said, "When the women
of this country ask for the ballot they should
have it," and as sure as truth is truth, so sure
now is the time to ask for it. Already in many
States they are voting on the school question,
and we need only to have that suffrage extend-
ed. But will the women in their new political
condition render the body politic no better and
purer? They certainly will, and that quickly.
In other words, before we attempt to govern
others we must show that we have a capacity
for self-government. If the laws are bad, we
must be willing to obey them until we can make
them better. If the ballot is given to the women
of this country, let them show the people that
they are fit descendants of the forefathers who
planned the revolution of 1776. Revolutions
make and unmake men, but no single mind
can control the impulse that drives them for-
ward. Slowly but surely in the last half century
has woman been coming to the front, and soon
she will be placed side by side with man as his
equal in everything. The era of woman is now.
She has only to put forth her hand to grasp the
forbidden fruits of the past. What she wants
is thorough and liberal education. The sole ob-
ject of the women of the next generation will
not be to get married, but they will fit them-
selves for self-support. The girl must be educat-
ed like the boy to trades and professions, and the
world is gradually waking up to this fact. In
Europe, as well as in America, the universities
now practice co-education, which gives a new
impetus to artistic and literary pursuits. When
men and women stand equal in everything,there
will be but one code of morals for man and for
woman. Hitherto, men in marrying women
have demanded of them that virtue which they
themselves could not return. See ye not, oh,
woman, that the harvests are ripe and the la-
borers few? Then, indeed, it is time for you to
be up and doing. Show your individuality and
that you are necessary for the perpetuation of
the government. There can be no elevated wo-
manhood without a noble manhood, and equal
and exact justice for both. We want great states-
men in this country rather than great generals,
and intelligent and thoughtful women rather
than leaders of fashion. If women are to have
the ballot, as I believe they are, they should ac-
cept it as a sacred trust, and use it accordingly.
If we want the ballot, now is the time to strike
the blow, but we must have united and concert-
ed action all along the line. Already have Eliza-
beth Cady Stanton, Susan B. Anthony and Lu-
cretia Mott seen the promised land, and I pray
to God that they may be allowed to enter in.
(Continued applause.)
	The speaking was concluded by a lengthy and
carefully-prepared address by Miss Phoebe Couz-
ins. Lack of space prevents a full report, and a
synopsis would do a decided injustice to such a
literary and logical effort."

NOTES AND COMMENTS FROM THE INDIANAPOLIS
JOURNAL.

	By far the liveliest scene in the convention
was that at the election of corresponding sec-
retary the first day. The committee on nomina-
tions reported in favor of the choice of Rachel G.
Foster, an intelligent and earnest young lady of
Philadelphia, and on the motion to amend the
report by substituting the name of Mrs. Sara
Andrews Spencer, of Washington, who has held
the office for some years, a spirited discussion

[Column 2]
ensued. The committee claimed that they had
decided upon the change because they under-
stood Mrs. Spencer's health was not good, and
they did not desire to impose upon her further.
That lady pertinently suggested that when she
wanted to be relieved of the office she would let
the convention know it, and not before, besides,
her health was as good as anybody's. She was
enthusiastically supported by "Father Adkin-
son, the prehistoric suffragist," and a number of
the older members of the association. Miss Fos-
ter, who had done nothing to secure the nomi-
nation, very sensibly said nothing, and left her
claims in the hands of her friends, and there, as
is usually the result they remained, as she was
defeated by a decided majority. Mrs. Spencer
is known to be earnest and self-sacrificing in the
work, and has made an excellent officer, but her
opponents claim that she is almost too arbitrary
in her management of affairs.
	The re-election of Elizabeth Cady Stanton to
the office of President of the Association is a de-
served compliment, although it was done over
her protest. In her letter to Miss Anthony she
stated that she was in favor of the choice of Mrs.
Z. G. Wallace of this city, for president of the
Association, as she possessed all the qualifica-
tions of a first-class executive officer, besided be-
ing thoroughly in earnest in the work.
	Mrs. Belva A. Lockwood, the distinguished
woman attorney of Washington City, arrived in
time for the night session Wednesday. In conver-
sation with a journal reporter last night she
said that she was detained in Washington by
important legal business and could not get here
sooner. When interrogated concerning the
Hill-Raymond case she said after attending the
Chicago convention she was going to Georgia
to collect testimony for the case. What the ul-
timate result of the trial will be she could not
say, but believed that it would be against Hill.
She said she had no interest in the matter ex-
cept that of an attorney, and as such she was
determined to press it to the end. Although,
she said, Miss Raymond had somewhat com-
promised herself by accepting money from Hill,
yet the case would be carried through all the
courts.
	In personal appearance Mrs. Lockwood is
rather large, with an excellent face and gray
hair, which is rolled above her forehead. She
was dressed in black silk and satin, and wore a
large gray felt hat, which gave her a decidedly
masculine appearance. In conversation she is
fluent and entertaining.
		*		*		*		*
	Miss Phoebe Couzins is probably the most at-
tractive looking lady in the convention, and
dresses richly and in perfect taste. She is an
excellent member of the Association, and her ad-
dress, delivered last night, was a masterly liter-
ary effort, one that any man might be proud to
have written.


RELIEF SOCITY, Y. L. M. I. A. AND
			PRIMARY REPORTS.
				
	Minutes of the Seventh Quarterly Conference
of the  Relief Societies of Utah Stake, held in the 
Provo  Meeting-house,  May  28th,  1880.  Meet-
ing  opened  by singing and  prayer. Minutes of 
preceding meeting read  and  approved.  The re-
ports  for the quarter  just ended  were read, also 
Primary reports. 
	President Mrs. M. T. Smoot said:  Although it
might seem  to some tedious to listen to so many 
reports, still they were of great importance. That
was what had called  us  together,  to  hear and 
learn of the progress  of  all.  The  Primary was 
a part of ourselves.  Time was far spent and pro-
fitably spent.  She  was  thankful that  she was 
able to meet with the societies again.  Although
her body was feeble, yet her spirit was vigorous. 
Realized that  God  was  very  good  to us.  Her
happiness was  increased  in  seeing so many  to-
gether,  and  as  we  were  favored  with having 
Brother Woodruff,  Sister  Phebe  Woodruff  and
Brother Teasdale with us, would hear the various
verbal reports  from the presidents of the differ-
ent wards and  hasten  our  business, in order to 
hear from  our  visitors.  The  presidents  gave
very interesting  accounts  of  the  doings and la-
bors of their wards,  showing zeal in the cause 
they have engaged in.
	Counselor John  was  very  much  gratified in 
listening to  the  progress  made  by her sisters.
Thought  the reports spoke  well  for  the  settle-
ments.  The speaker said we should be thankful
that we could by our feeble efforts do something to 
promote the welfare of Zion.  Prayed God to bless
all who took  an active part in Zion's cause.


[Column 1]
		BOX ELDER STAKE. 

	SISTERS' CONFERENCE, 
			Tuesday, June 18, 1880.
	Mrs. M. Snow presiding. Opening prayer,
Mrs. S. P. Booth. Among those present were
Sisters E. R. Snow Smith and E. B. Wells, of
Salt Lake City. Minutes and condensed report
of March Conference read. Sister Eliza blessed
the sisters with comforting words. E. B. Wells
encouraged us with her eloquence. S. P. Booth
reported first ward. Upon motion conference
adjourned until 2 p.m.
	Afternoon. Presidents of Relief Societies
gave verbal reports, which were followed by re-
marks by Uncle Joseph Young, the sisters from
Salt Lake City and Apostle L. Snow, all re-
minding us of our many duties and pointing out
the path of salvation. Benediction by Bro.
Isaac Smith.
	June 10th. Devoted to Y. L. M. I. A., the
superintendent presiding. All presidents pre-
sent reported verbally. Remarks were then
made by other officers, all expressing a deter-
mination to improve.
	2 p.m. Dedicated to the presidents of Primary
Association to report verbally.
	5 p.m. The little folks' conference. Each
one of the four hundred present felt their pres-
ence indispensable. Upon the whole our con-
ference is one long to be remembered with grate-
ful feelings and many thanks to our dearly be-
loved Sister Eliza, and not forgetting others of
our sister visitors. Conference adjourned until
September 10th. Benediction by Lizze Felt.
	Financial, Cash and Property:
Receipts, 	-	-	-		$235,43.
		Disbursements:
	To Poor, $30.70; Temple, 30.05; Emigration,
29.25; Missionary, 11.50; Lamanites, 9.25; Build-
ings, 49.25; Home Industries, 90.05; Sta-
tionery, 6.19; Wheat, 44 bushels.
	Total on hand, 	-	-	-	$289,57.
						M. E. SNOW, 
					Recording Secretary. 

	The Young Ladies Mutual Improvement As-
sociation of the 8th Ward, Salt Lake City, met
as usual, June 25th, 1880, and were highly edi-
fied in listening to a lecture from Mrs. Dr. R. B.
Pratt. The Dr. said she would not give all she
would like to in one short lecture, but would
try and glean what would interest the young
ladies, which did so, and all felt very well paid
for attending. Before finishing Dr. Pratt ex-
horted the young ladies to study and learn in
regard to their health, felt pleased with the in-
terest of the listeners, said she would be pleased
at any time to meet with us. The Association
feel to thank Dr. Pratt for her lecture and sin-
cerely hope to hear from her again.
	We hold our meetings now once a month.
Our exercises consist of reading, select recita-
tions, songs, music, and teachings from various
members.
	We are pleased at all times to see visitors, and
cordially invite our good sisters who are working
for the improvement and elevation of the
daughters of Zion. 
				MARY S. LEAVER, President,
				S. A. HAWKINS, Secretary.

			BEAVER REPORT.
	The first Sisters' Conference held in the Bea-
ver Stake of Zion, in May last, had a good re-
presentation from every settlement in the coun-
ty. Sister Lucinda Howd presiding. Singing
by the choir, prayer by Elder Robertson. There
were many representative brethren and sisters
on the stand from the different settlements.
President Howd first stated the object of the 
Conference and then the reports were read from
the different places, representing all the societies
in a prosperous condition, temporally and spirit-
ually. President John Ashworth, Elder Wm.
Fotheringham, M. A. McDonny and L. Skinner
spoke of the many good principles pertaining to
our religion. D. F. Cox spoke of the
the many advantages the young people of the
present time enjoyed for improvement  compar-
ed to what we older ones had in our younger
days; exhorted the sisters to faithfulness in per-
forming all the duties enjoined upon them as
Latter-day Saints. Bro. Robertson spoke very
instructively and edifyingly, being filled with
the power of God. A good spirit prevailed
throughout the entire meeting, and every one
who spoke, both men and women, testified to

[Column 2]
the same. Bro. Blackburn spoke upon Celestial
marriage, said we should teach this principle
to our children. Many of the sisters
gave much good and glorious instruction and
bore powerful testimonies of what they had seen
and heard, which corroborated the truth of the 
everlasting Gospel. Sister Mary E. Lightner
went back in her remarks to the days of the
Prophet Joseph, and related many things plea-
sant to hear. Sister L. B. Pratt, our aged veter-
an of the cross, spoke of many interesting inci-
dents of her life; and said she was living on bor-
rowed time, and at her advanced age she had
learned not to fear man but to put her trust in
God. I will just say there is not power in my
pen to write the many good things that were
said in our Conference.
	Brothers Ashworth and Fotherignham said
they never enjoyed themselves better than dur-
ing this conference. They would not say the 
sisters had done some good preaching lest they
should not like it, but they would say they had
done some very good talking. And the presi-
dents of the young ladies gave some very good
and intelligent instructions. 
	Jane Gillis, president of the Primary Associa-
tion, and her counselors, L. Shepherd and Irene
Hills, presented their little intelligent band with
instructions. The secretary called the roll, and
the minutes of the P. A. were read by Edna
Farnsworth, a little girl 11 years of age. The
young are improving greatly in qualifications
in Beaver. The sisters are much united in
keeping their covenants and living up to their
duties. Sister Howd presided with great dig-
nity. 			                     RACHEL.


WOMAN'S  EXPONENT.


[Column 1]	
	Mrs. Woodruff said she was truly pleased in
having the privilege of meeting with the sisters
of Utah County. She had listened with interest
to the account given of the faithful labors of the 
sisters of this Stake. We had been organized
for a noble work.  Let us not yield to the powers
of darkness by neglecting  our  prayers,  but  with
all  our  energies assist in  our small  way  to  roll
on  the glorious work of building temples that we
may have the privilege of entering in and doing
a work for our dear friends, who are waiting the
other side of the vail for us to assist them.  Mrs.
Woodruff said through faith and prayer she had
received a testimony of  the truth of  the gospel,
and urged the necessity  of  all  having  the testi-
mony of Jesus within them, that their faith fail
not.  Let us arrange our home affairs to meet as
often as possible, that our spirits may be fed by
the spirit of  God,  then  we  will  rejoice  in  the
trials we are called  to pass through.  The expe-
rience we gain here will be of untold value to us.
May God bless my dear sisters,  and  I  earnestly
ask him to bless  Sister  Smoot.  I  have known
her many years as a tried and faithful friend.
	Mrs. Lucky W. Kimball presented the compli-
ments of  Miss  E.  R. Snow, S. M. Kimball and
E. B. Wells, who had been invited to meet with
us.  Through press of business  they could not
come, but desired  to  extend  their sincere love,
and felt that they would be with the conference
in spirit.  Mrs. Kimball made an earnest appeal
in behalf of the EXPONENT.  Was confident that
all present could testify  that the spirit of God
had been with us during our conference.  Sister
M. T. Smoot also spoke in behalf of our Woman's-
Paper.
	Adjourned until 2  p.m. Singing, benediction by 
Bishop Paxman. 
	Two o'clock p.m. After the opening  exer-
cises, the  president  said  we  will  now  hear
from Brother Woodruff.  "My sisters and moth-
ers in Zion—I am glad to meet with you to-day.
The Relief  Society  was  organized  by  Joseph
Smith by revelation, and  has  for  its  object the
building up of the Kingdom of God.  It does not
all depend  upon  the  Elders  of  Israel  by  any
means.   You  are  like  ministering  angels; you
are aids to the bishops, and a great many worthy
people would suffer were  it  not  for these organ-
izations.  The responsibilities of building up this
Kingdom  rests  alike  upon  the  man  and  the
woman.  While the Elders  are  among the na-
tions preaching the Gosepl, the mothers have the
guidance of the children.  When I contemplate
the positions of you mothers in bearing the souls
of men, and your future destiny, it is far beyond
the  honors  conferred  upon  the  queens  of the
earth, who are looked upon  with honor.  When
they die  there is an end of their glory.  Your
promise is eternal.  You cannot know the result
of your labors until  you pass behind the vail.
You have no thrones here, but when  you have
faithfully performed the work you were ordained
to do on this earth, your exaltation will be eter-
nal, you  will  inherit  kingdoms forever. It is
only a step to the other  side, it  is  like  passing
from one door to another, and what little time
you remain here  do  all the  good  you can, train
your children aright, teach them the principles
of life and salvation, try to  stem the tide of evil
that is gaining a foothold among our young peo-
ple, teach your children to honor the  principles
of marriage as  revealed  through  the  Prophet
Joseph, without this order of marriage we cannot
be exalted; instill these eternal  principles in the
minds of  the  rising  generation, that they may
control their actions through life.  Let me  say
to the sisters of Zion, we cannot do without your
assistance in accomplishing this great work, our
sons and daughters are to bear off this kingdom,
and it devolves upon  the  mothers  to  prepare
them for the "work before them."  The speaker
said he had never been at home but one summer
in his life, of course the training of children  was
entirely out of the question.  One of his children
was over a year old  before  he  saw him.  "Be
faithful my sisters, that when  the  trump shall
sound, we may come forth and inherit eternal
lives."
	Brother Teasdale  made  some  excellent  re-
marks in regard to the vast amount of goed done
by the Relief Societies,  and  the  object of their
mission. Brother John said he had been highly
 edified and he hoped the sisters would give heed
to the advice of Brother Woodruff.
	Conference  adjourned  until  August  27th.
After singing.  Prayer by Brother Teasdale.
	Statistical and Financial Report:  Statistical—
No. of members,  2,031;  No.  of Teachers,  329;
No. of meetings held,  156;  average  attendance
626;  church  papers  taken,  321;  EXPON-
ENTS, 117. 
	Financial:—Total receipts,  $743;  total disburs-

[Column 2]
ments, $653; to poor, $401; to  Temple, $77.55; for
building  purposes,  $113.63;  missionary,  $9.04;
home industries, $28.50;  P.  E.  Fund, $21; book
and stationery, $2.35; cash on hand, $261.26; pro-
perty on hand,  $7,134.20;  wheat  loaned,  1,101
bushels and 32  pounds;  wheat  on  hand,  1,053
bushels and 27 pounds. 
	CAROLINE DANIELS, Sec'y
	LUCY W. KIMBALL, Cor. Sec'y
			
	KANAB.
	The Quarterly Conference of  the Relief Soci-
eties of the Kanab Stake was held  at   Glendale,
June 4th, 1880, Counselor Mrs. Harriet  Bunting
presiding.  Present on the stand:  Counselors H.
Bunting and L. A. Johnson;  Sister L. Spencer,
president of the  Orderville  Ward,  and  Sister
Foot, of the Glendale Ward.  Of  the  brethren:
Presidents H. C. Spencer and Jas. L. Bunting,
Bishops  W.  D.  Johnson, Jr.,  Royal Cutler, T.
Smith, and Elder Carpenter.  The brethren and
sisters made  some very  interesting  remarks in
reference to home manufature, the responsibil-
ities of parents in  rearing  their children;  urged
them to give the little ones  good  educations, to
teach  them  politeness,  and  impress  on their
young minds the importance of  prayer and obe-
dience to the priesthood; wished  the  sisters to
do what they could to help gather the poor.  In
fact, the whole meeting was replete  with  good
counsel  and  instruction.  The  conference was
adjourned till September 3d,  to be  held at Ka-
nab. The Societies  in  the  Stake have loaned
their wheat to the needy, as desired by President
Taylor.  The sisters are trying  to  live their reli-
gion and  to do the best they can, in the absence
of President Elizabeth Nuttall, whom  we  hope
will soon be with us again.  Wishing success to
the EXPONENT and peace and prosperity to Zion,
	I remain, your sister in the gospel,
	M. ELIZABETH LITTLE, Secretary.

	The Primary Association of Levan  was  orga-
nized May 29th, 1879, by Sister  Eliza R. Snow
and Sister M. I. Horne.  We held our first an-
niversary May 29th, 1880;  after the usual  open-
ing exercises, Sister Esther Gardner made a few
remarks and read the report  of  our  Association
and exercises of the past year; the  children  re-
cited pieces and sang songs, also recited the  Ar
ticles of Faith; eight of the children repeated the 
ten  commandments,  and  answered  questions
from  them,  and  from  the  Juvenile  Instsuctor.
Bishop Aagard and H. Peterson gave some very
encouraging remarks.  Meetings held, 40; names
enrolled,  78; average  attendance,  33;  financial
report, donations  received,  $5.50, and 50 cts in
calico.  Donated to Manti Temple $3.00; expend-
ed for the Association, $4.90;  remaining  in  the
treasury,  60  cents, and  one quilt  made by  the
Primary Association, for the Temple, price $5.00.
Singing by the children, "Jesus, Mighty King in
Zion." benediction by Bishop Aagrad. Esther
Gardner, President; Eliza  Curtis,  Anna  Peter-
son,  Counselors;  Martha  Gardner,  Secretary;
Amelia  Mangelson,  Assistant;  Irinda  Taylor,
Treasurer.

	The Y. L. M. I. A. of Paradise, Cache County,
organized under the direction of sisters Eliza R.
Snow and Elizabeth  Davis, Aug. 10,  '78,  with
the following officers:
	Carrie M. C. Smith, President; Elizabeth San-
diford, Sarah Ann Obray, Elizabeth Remington,
Henrietta Sinfield, Counselors: Mary J. Oldham,
Secretary; and Harriet  Howells,  Assistant-Sec-
retary.  They still  remain the same  with  the
exception of the secretaries, who have been  ab-
sent from the settlement of some time. Their
places are now filled by Sisters  Fanny  Gibbs as
Secretary and Ellen Bishop Assistant-Secretary,
also adding to our number Charlotte  Ann Loft-
house, as Treasurer.  We have enrolled seventy
members,  with an  average  attendance  of  45.
November last we subscribed for the EXPONENT
which is ever  welcome and  appreciated  by  the
members.  We hold  our  meetings  weekly,   on
Tuesday evening.  Also conjoint meetings  with
the Y. M. M. I. A., monthly.  They are con-
ducted,  on a whole, the same as  proposed  by
Brother Wells and Hardy, which method is en-
tered into heartily and attended with  good  suc-
oess.  We have not  progressed  in  a  temporal
point of  view  so much as could be desired; our
circumstances not being so favorable.  Of one
thing  we  feel confident, that we have  kept the
object of these organizations, viz.:  Improvement
rather than entertainment. We feel also that the
spirit enjoyed  by the  members is mainly  due to
the  inspiring  visit of Sister Snow  and  Davis.
We feel that they have touched a chord  in  the
hearts of the sisters that will never cease to vi-

[Column 3]
brate.  We will close our communication,  desir-
ing to be remembered by the  remainder  of  our
sisters in the cause of truth.
	FANNY GIBBS, Sec'y
			
	The Quarterly Conference  of  the  Y. L. M. I.
Association of the  Weber  Stake  of  Zion  was
held in the Tabernacle, Ogden, June 18th, Presi-
dent Sarah Herrick presiding. Meeting opened
by singing, "Now  let  us  Rejoice."  Prayer by
Bishop Hart.  Singing, "May we who know the
Joyful  Sound."  Minutes  of  last  Conference
read  and  accepted.  Reports  were  then  read
from thirteen associations, all being reported ex-
cept one.  President  Sarah  Herrick  addressed
the meeting.  She was  pleased with the reports
and was satisfied the  young  ladies were awake
and trying to  perform,  to  the  utmost of their
ability, the  duties  assigned  them.  Felt inte-
rested in the great  work  in  which we were en-
gaged,  and  was  desirous  of  doing  more good.
Hoped we would all profit  by  the good instruc-
tion given at the R. S.  Conference  yesterday,
and ever strive to do  the  will  of  our  Father in
heaven.
	Bishop Sanford Bingham  exhorted the young
ladies  to  try  and  fully  appreciate  the  great
blessings they receive and improve every oppor-
tunity to gain  wisdom  and  knowledge,  that in
after years they might  have  nothing  to regret,
President Sarah Richards, Summit Stake, spoke
on the training of children, necessity of prayer,
liberality, and the despicable practice of tattling.
	Bishop Hart,  West  Weber,  gave  much good
instruction in regard to our duties as Saints, and
was sure if we  lived  our  religion  all  would be
well.
	Prest.  Jane  S. Richards  followed,  endorsing
what had been said, and  impressing  upon  the
minds of the young ladies the necessity of hav-
ing great  faith  and  of  being  united.  Advised
mothers to teach their children when young the
principles of the gospel, and  the  importance of
attending to their prayers and other duties faith-
fully.  Spoke on various other subjects and was
listened to throughout  with  marked  attention.
Meeting adjourned  till  2 p.m. Singing, "Do
what is right."  Benediction by  Elder Thomas
Morley.
	2 p.m.  After singing  and  prayer, Mrs. Mar-
tha Critchelow, president R. S. First Ward was
called upon to address the meeting.  She bore a 
faithful testimony to the  truth  of  the  religion
she had embraced, had been  richly  paid for  at-
tending this conference,  and  hoped  all  present
would try and carry out the  good counsel which 
had been given.
	Mrs.  Martha  Bingham,  president  R.  S.  of 
Riverdale, said many  good  things  encouraging
to all who are striving to  do  right, gave much
excellent counsel and asked the Lord to bless all
in their efforts to do good.
	The presidents of  the  Young Ladies Associa-
tions present each spoke, expressing  a feeling of
deep interest in the associations over which they
were placed, and a desire to perform their duties
acceptably, relying upon their  heavenly Father
for  strength,  knowing  they  were  incom-
petent  to  fill  such  responsible  posi-
tions  without  his  spirit  to  guide   them.
Interesting and instructive remarks were made
by counselors,  Mrs.  Emily  S.  Richards, Mrs.
Josephine R. West and Mrs. Letitia Peery, which
were calculated in their  nature, to  inspire the
young ladies to  greater  dilligence and renewed
efforts in the  cause  of  mutual  improvement.
Bishop McQuarrie gave  a very earnest address,
speaking upon many principles of  our holy reli-
gion, pointing out clearly the importance of ob-
serving the Word of Wisdom, and setting an ex-
ample worthy of  imitation.  Prayed  the bless-
ings of heaven to rest upon all  who are interest-
ed in building up the kingdom  of  God  on  the
earth.  President Sarah  Herrick  had  enjoyed
the Conference, and trusted all felt blessed and
 benefitted by the good counsel they had received.
Exhorted the young ladies to  persevere in good
works,  and  perform  faithfully  every  known
duty.  Conference was then adjourned for three
months.
	Statistical  and  financial  report;  statistical:
members, 620; meetings held, 96;  average at-
tendance, 2,863;  conjoint  sessions,  34;  visits of
county officers,  9;  chapters  read  in  Church
Works, 8,442; lectures delivered, 32; testimonies
born, 178.
	Financial: Cash on hand at date of last report,
$64.70;  cash  received, $82.57;  disbursed for chari-
table  purposes, books, etc., $104.53;  amount  of
cash and  property on  hand,  $62.79.  There is
also some wheat which was not reported.
	ROSE CANFIELD, Secretary.

PRINCESS Louise has sent to England a box of

ture, as a contribution to the Cheadle Bazaar
opened at Cheadle Rdctory.

	AT an annual meeting of Friends in Philadel-
phia, the renowned veteran lady Lucretia Mott
now in her 88th year lifted her sweet and elo-
qunt voice strongly against light litrature, which
is now doing such extensive injury to the minds
of the young. Would that brave strong words
like these could be sounded like a trumpet in the
ears of those who devote their best days to such
like occupation.

WOMAN'S EXPONENT.

The Rights of the Women of Zion, and the Rights of the Women of all Nations.

VOL. 9.		SALT LAKE CITY, UTAH, AUGUST 1, 1880.		No. 5.


[Column 1]
FAITH AND WORKS.

	"Faith cometh by hearing the Word of God.—Doc-
trine and Covenants.

See! the wilds, so long forsaken, into life and bloom
	awaken—
'Tis the meed of Faith unshaken, the reward of labor
	too.
Faith hath wrought this exultation, for the "outcasts'
	of the nation;
Yea, through Faith "God favors Zion"—Faith and Works
	can wonders do.

Ah, this Faith! Can words express it? Can the jeers of
	foes suppress it?
'Tis superior to language, far above reproach and scorn;
'Tis indeed the blest assurance, that for patient, brief
	endurance,
We shall reap the full fruition of the hopes within us
	born,

'Tis in vain men cry "delusion," souls are thrilled with
	Faith's infusion,
Faith reanimates the spirit, as the life blood cheers the
	heart;
Needful 'tis that we obtain it, needful 'tis that we re-
	tain it—
Though we never can explain it, Fath doth power and
	peace impart.

Fath's the fruit of revelation, Faith's the anchor of
	salvation:
Faith obtains from God a knowledge of the truth that
	cheers the soul;
Faith's the true appreciation of Christ's love and media-
	tion;
Faith's the force of Truth within us, Faith's the power
	that makes us whole.

For this Faith it is no wonder, men have e'en been torn
	asunder,
Men have "cru'lly been tormented," scorning to accept
	reprieve,
Knowing (though by fiends surrounded) that in truth
	their faith was founded—
Scorn'd they to deny for freedom what they could not
	but believe;

By the ladder of affliction—sword, and fire, and cruci-
	fixion—
For their Faith, by deaths most tortuous noblest souls
	have upward soar'd—
Passed these martys up to glory, leaving us their death-
	less story,
While they cry, How long, Thou just One, ere Thy
	vengeance is outpour'd?

Of eternal condemnation there's a fearful reservation
For the murderers of these just ones, of these brave,
	illustrious dead!
Read we from the sacred pages, how that from remotest
	ages,
From the death of "righteous Abel," many for their
	faith have bled.So, within this generation, by a free and favor'd nation,
Prophets have for faith been murder'd, men have sorely
	been opprest;
For their Faith—through much privation—"sought they
	out a habitation,"
Even in a distant desert, in the wild, uncultured west.
Sheltered by majestic mountains, lo! they drank from
	freedom's fountains;
Blest with liberty, they-labor'd, 'reft of much that men
	desire;
Faith, by willing hands assisted, every obstacle resisted.
Faith in God and in the future still these exiles hearts
	inspire.

Faith shall make the Saints victorious, Faith shall make
	them great and glorious;
God Himself will watch keep o'er us, righteous laws
	we'll still obey;
Men may form their own deductions, why we honor God's
	instructions,

[Column 2]
Still we'll pray for Faith to serve Him though opposing
	hosts say nay—
Faith to brave the world's opinion, till the glory and
	dominion
And the greatness of "the Kingdom" shall be given the
	Saints for aye.
					EMILY HILL WOODMANSEE.
	S. L. City, July 24, 1880.


[Column 1]
PEN SKETCH OF AN ILLUSTRIOUS
WOMAN.

ELIZA R. SNOW SMITH.

	This eminent and highly cultured lady is
perhaps more widely known,here and abroad,
than any women in the Church of Latter-day
Saints. She occupies the highest position
among the sisters, and may with all propriety
be designated, "The Elect Lady." Sister Eliza
is a woman of the strongest mental powers; of
sterling and unflinching integrity, and is val-
iant for the cause of truth and liberty. In fact
no better type can be given of historically famous
Pilgrim mothers, than the remarkable woman
whose life and works form the subject of this
sketch. She possesses in a high degree the
same striking energy of character, tenacity of
purpose and perseverance under accumulated
difficulties as the heroic foremothers of New
England, from whom she is descended, Indus-
try and economy have gone hand in hand with
her through life. She may justly be termed a
disciplinarian; is ever punctual to a moment,
has order very largely developed and remark-
able power of concentration, combined with
excellent ability; these with almost perfect self-
control, great decision of character and a reso-
lute will make her pre-eminently a leader. Jus-
tice is one of her strong points and she often
says "Justice before mercy."	In any age or in any country she must have
been a superior woman, but with the peculiar
experiences of Mormonism, she is lifted far
above anything she could possibly have at-
tained in the world. One of the most powerful
attributes of her nature is a sublime reverence
for the Deity and all that pertains to religion
and an exalted life. This combined with her
other qualifications for leadership peculiarly fit
her for the high and holy callings of Priestess
and Mother in Israel in the new and last dis-
pensation. Not only has she been elected by
the people and ordained and set apart to ex-
pound the Scriptures and preside over the
sisters in their various organizations by the
Servant of God, who holds the office of Prophet,
Seer and Revelator, to the Church of Christ
here upon the earth, but she has been baptized
as by fire and sanctified through suffering of
the keenest and severest character. These try-
ing ordeals have lifted her into the atmosphere
of a higher life, and consecrated and endowed
her for her ministerial work in the broadest
fields of labor, the hearts and homes of the
people.
	Think not gentle friends that this exclusive
devotion to the interests of the kingdom of God,
bringing this heroic woman as it does constantly
out before the public,is unaccompanied by daily
sacrifice. Her heart has the same holy aspira-
tions, the same tender womanly sympathies and
appreciations of the enjoyments of home and its
solid attractions, as that of her sister woman;
but these must not, she will not suffer them to
come between her and her life-work. She is
wedded to her sacred and holy mission: to

[Column 2]
minister to humanity in every condition of life
where woman can comfort, bless, sustain and
elevate.
	I have said so much of Sister Eliza not for
those who know her intimately, but who hear
of her as a representative woman and know
little of her real self and her individual life.
	Eliza Roxie Snow was born in Becket, Berk-
shire Co. Mass. Her parents were Oliver Snow
of Mass., and Rosetta Leonora Pettibone of
Conn. They were of English descent their
forefathers emigrating to America at a very
early period. In 1806 Mr. Snow moved with
his family to Ohio, and settled in Mantua
Portage Co. Being a man of sound judgment
and good ability as well as education he filled
several offices of emolument and trust in the
gift of the people, his daughter Eliza at quite
an early age officiating as his secretary. Mrs.
Snow like a wise and judicious mother deter-
mined her daughters should be instructed in
everything pertaining to domestic life and its
duties, and consequently Eliza though naturally
a gifted child and inclined to literary pursuits
was early initiated in all the routine and small-
est details of housekeeping, as well as plain and
ornamental needle work of which she still pre-
serves many rare specimens. She also learned
straw-braiding and was awarded two prizes at
the County Fair for the manufacture of the
best leghorn. Through this sort of training
she became practically selfhelpful. While quite
young Miss Snow commenced writing for the
press, and contrary to most young writers, her
poems were well received. She wrote for sev-
eral journals using a fictitious signature desir-
ing to be useful, but modestly shrinking from
public notice. Through writing a patriotic
poem at the time of the struggle between
Greece and Turkey she created quite a sensa-
tion. It was entitled "The Fall of Missilong-
hi;" and after the death of those famous states-
men Adams and Jefferson, which occurred on
the same day, she was requested through the
press to write their requiem. Shortly after she
received for a prize poem, eight volumes of
Godey's Lady's Book. Miss Snow's poems
have ever been of a prophetic and inspirational
character, and after partaking of the spirit of
the true Gospel she became more than before
richly endowed with the gift of poesy, inspira-
tion and prophecy, and her hymns and songs
have been such a feature of beauty in the
Church, that from an early period in its history
she has borne the title of "Poetess of Zion."
	We shall not attempt to give even in brief a
synopsis of the lady's writings—suffice it to say
she has immortalized her name, and crowned
with wreaths of honor, she might justly say
with the immortal Byron in his famous prayer
"I've lived the laurel wreath to gain." Her
poems have given comfort to the weary, encour-
agement to the weak and those whose burdens
were heavy and whose trials were grievous;
they have strengthened the hands and hearts
of pioneer men and women while enduring
severe hardships. They have stimulated the
young to nobler and higher purposes. Histori-
cally they have been of the greatest import-
ance. Through them the names of many of
the actors in the drama of Mormonism, will
be handed down to posterity. There has
scarcely been an event in the history of the
Church which has not been faithfully deline-
ated by her gifted pen. She was to use her
own expression, born a patriot, "she had that
sort of blood in her veins. Her grandfather


had been a revolutionary soldier; and when
quite young, she listened with intense and child-
ish eagerness to his thrilling, adventurous nar-
ratives of the war with the British. She has
written many patriotic and national poems,
Anthems and Choruses.
	In her writings both in prose and poetry, she
manifests a striking versatility of genius and a
happy faculty of adaptation to the various cir-
cumstances and conditions of human life at
times participating in the deepest sentiments of
sorrow and also with sublime ideality feasting
upon the perfection of enjoyment.
	Two volumes of her "Religious, Historical,
Political," poems have been published–the First
in Liverpool, England, in 1856, the Second in
Salt Lake City by the Deseret News Office,
which has just issued her Hymn Book for the
Primary Associations, which contains 20 hymns
from her pen, the others she has carefuly com-
piled from various authors,many from our own
poets—the remainder from foreign publications.
Sterotyped 1st. 7000 Edition.
	Her poems are true to life.—Those in her
published volumes addressed to pioneers and
those written during the drivings and journey-
ings of the Saints from place to place are some
very pathetic;—they come vividly home to the
hearts of the L. D. Saints. Two of this class
in Vol. 1, written after we left Nauvoo in the
bleak winter season, entitled "Camp of Israel"
describe minutely the condition of the Pilgrim
Saints. We should never let our children for-
get them. Poems like these make deeper and
more lasting impressions upon the memory
than whole volumes of history.

To be Continued.


34		WOMAN'S EXPONENT.
[Column 1]
had a friend in whom she reposed great con-
fidence, living in a town about thirty miles
away, the wife of a clergyman, who had no
daughter of her own, and whose superior edu-
cation and accomplishments would be a great
advantage to Hepsie in developing her un-
formed character, and so 'twas settled at home
with the family, and preparations were rapidly 
made for the journey. Letters were inter-
changed between Mrs. H. and Mrs. G., and
the latter was given instructions in regard to 
discipilining the mind and character of the
eccentric child whose guidance she was about
to undertake. The new dresses and other
wearing apparel awakened but little interest
in this unsophisticated child, but the thought
of leaving the dear old places, the familiar
scenes where she had spent her happy child-
hood almost overcame her and she wept bitter-
ly when she was reminded that she was going
among strangers. However, she was just 
beginning to learn that life was not all made
up of fancies, and indeed the death of her
grandfather had worked a radical change in 
the tenor of her thoughts. She was more
serious and contemplative and earth was not
quite so rosy-hued nor the future so unreal as
heretofore. 
	One delightful May morning when the
lilacs were in bloom, and the fragrance of the
apple blossoms sweet upon the air, she dressed
herself with more than usual care, for this was 
to be her advent out into the great world,
albeit only to an adjoining town, whither she
had never been. She tripped lightly down
the stairs though her heart was sad, and out 
into the grove at the back to say adieu to the
trees and rocks, and brook for she had loved
them all with a childish simplicity. She
gathered a bunch of violets and pinning
them daintily on her black dress, she went in 
to say good-bye to the brothers and sisters 
weeping bitterly all the time. Her breakfast
was untasted, though the choicest morsels had 
been prepared for her, and a nice lunch for
the journey. Gloomy forebodings were in her
mind at the thoughts of leaving her dear ones, 
and going forth alone. She had never seen
Mrs. G., and the idea she formed of her was
not at all prepossessing. Finally the moment
came, the sound of the carriage wheels and
the lumbering old coach drove up, Hepsie
kissed them all good-bye and climbed into the
stage, her eyes full of tears and her throat
with sobs; the driver cracked his whip with a 
great flourish, and the four fine horses darted
forward down the road. Looking out back of 
the coach window, she saw her stately undemon-
strative mother going up the lilac walk slowly, 
and wiping her eyes with her apron. 
	Hepsie was rather glad her mother wept, 
for she thought grown women never cried, 
except when some one was dead. Her own 
tears were falling fast, but childish grief is 
not of long duration, and she soon forgot her
trouble in the excitement of the journey, the
lovely scenery through which they were pass
ing and the strange faces around her. 
	There were two or three other passengers
and one old lady in green spectacles, who asked
Hepsie inquisitively where her mother was; 
then seeing the black crape on her dress she
said, "O your mother's dead, poor thing! and 
I s'pose your going to your rich relations."
"My mother isn't dead, and I'm going to 
school, and I haven't any relatives except my
uncle and cousins in Boston, and they are too 
proud to care for us! I hate my uncle any-
way, for he has locked up my grandpa's house, 
and says he'll have it pulled down; and that's 
the reason I'm sent away, because I cried so
much for grandpa and the old house."
	"Well, well to be sure what a chatterbox,"
and added the old lady, "you'll soon forget all
about it." "No, I sha'n't" said Hepsie, "he 
was the grandest man you ever saw;" "I never

[Column 2]
saw him," said the lady. "Well, if you ever
had seen him, you would think so, ane every-
body at the 'Old Furnace' thought so," "Well, 
I never," said the green spectacled lady again; 
"what are you talking about, the Old Fur
nace?" Was Major Hathaway your grand-
father, the man who owned the old factory at B?"
"Well, I do declare; no wonder you thought 
him grand, why everybody in these parts
knew him; and as for his wife, though she
died long ago, when I was a little girl I almost
worshiped her. She was a rare beauty, but
what an ugly name she had, Hephizbah."
"That's my name," said the child quickly, "but
they call me Hepsie nowadays?" "Well?
well! I never did," the old lady repeated
again and again, under her breath. "Why he
was very old, ninety-six or seven he must have
been."
	Hepsie had wakened early that morning and
she had become so exhausted with crying that 
after answering many more of the lady's in-
terrogations she fell fast asleep in the coach 
and forgot her great sorrow. 
	It was late in the afternoon ere they reached
the place of destination, and the old lady in 
the green spectacles had been put down at one
of the towns on the way. The stage driver had
been directed where to leave Hepsie, and ar-
riving at the parsonage just outside the centre 
of the town of M., he stopped and lifted the 
little girl out. Mrs. G. was in waiting, and
came out to meet her; the driver lifted down 
the little hair trunk, that contained Hepsie's
wardrobe and carried it up the steps. She 
had never been away from home alone before
and was so timid that she trembled with fright, 
besides she had been forewarned that she 
could not "put on airs" with Mrs. Grovesner. 
The lady inquired kindly if she were tired, 
and asked concerning her mother's health, and
then ushered her into a large, square, sitting-
room, so antique and curious in style, as to 
cause her to compare it with grandpa's dining-
room in the old, old house, from which she
was now as she believed shut out forever. 
Everything was bewilderingly strange. After
partaking some refreshment as it was not time
for "tea," Hepsie walked out into the garden, 
and there again she was reminded of the farm. 
The shrubbery was charming, and the apple-
blossoms, dainty perfume scented all the air; 
the ground was covered with pretty pink and
white leaves, and the garden seemed like a 
fairy bower. 
	The house itself viewing it from the out-
side was quite as romantic as the one she 
had loved so well, and Hepsie found her-
self wondering if the long dark passages
under the sloping sides of the gambrel-roof,
contained as many old papers as there was at
her grandfather's, and if so, whether Mrs. 
Grovesner would let her rummage them over, 
and sit and read all day in the garret as she
used to do sometimes in the past. When night
came the house seemed gloomy and Hepsie 
wondered and was awed by the stillness, for at 
home the children made merry music with 
their chatter and laughter. The minister for
the husband of Mrs. Grovesner was the Rev.
Erasmus Grovesner, looked very solemn and
spoke only to "Deborah," for so he designated
his wife, and Hepsie queried whether he loved
her, he spoke so coldly; for the child had heard
her grandpa talk a great deal about love, and
of his undying love for her grandmother; and
though she could not know exactly what love
meant, still it embodied her childish idea of
true happiness. Mrs. G. was occupied with
some plain needle work, and Hepsie scarcely
knew what to do with her hands; and was
very restless; besides she wanted to ask a lot
of questions, but she dare not for the silence
to her was something painful. Finally prayer
was offered in such a stately way, and not the
least impressive to the child, and Hepsie was 

[Column 3]
informed it was time for her to retire. Mrs.
Grovesner showed her to a little low chamber, 
a long way from the sitting room, and the
hall and stairways were so dark, that she 
shivered with fear for she was in some respects
a coward; and the stories of ghosts and giants
were vivid in her mind. The woman took no
notice of the child, she had been requested to 
discipline her, and considered this was the
proper course to take, and, it required firmness
and she was just the one to undertake such a 
task. 	
	The little room was still as the grave, 
and the bed looked as though no one had ever
slept in it, Mrs. G. put the candle down and 
said "good night," and in a moment Hopsie
fell to soliloquizing as usual, "I'm sure I can't 
sleep, what a dismal place this is, how quiet
and gloomy. So this is the beginning of the
discipline mother said Mrs. G. would give me;"
and by this time she had burst into tears, 
childish relief, and ofttimes a blessed relief to 
older children too, when the heart is full to 
overflowing. Poor Hepsie imagined herself
cruelly treated, and at once conceived the idea
of writing her life and making this the first 
chapter. She repeated over and over the mul-
tiplication table and then all the verses she
knew, and while thus engaged fell fast asleep. 
		[To be continued.]

THE LADY DOCTORS OF INDIA. 
	"The Englishman's Overland Mail" says:
In India lady doctors are now familiar to us, 
and although at first they may have been 
somewhat ridiculed by those who could not
appreciate their value, they are fast making
their presence felt for good almost in every
corner of the land. So far as the native
women of this country are concerned, it is 
gratifying to note that their success in all
branches of college education is progressing to 
the entire satisfaction of their professors. 
Not only have they proved themselves to be
generally well fitted for the arduous duties at-
tendant on medical studies, but they have in 
some cases succeeded beyond all ordinary ex-
pectation. Bombay, Madras, the North West
Provinces, and the Punjaub all return flatter-
ing reports on the subject, and when we say 
that a class of female students can average
over 700 marks out of 1,000 in a surgical ex-
amiantion, as we hear has recently been the
case, little can be said against their power, or
skill, or aptitude for gaining knowledge in one
of the most important branches of the medical
profession. Indeed, it appears not unlikely
that women in India may prove themselves by
no means inferior to men in most branches of
the practice of medicine, if the progress made
by native females in hospital work may be
taken as a criterion. In many cases they have
proved themselves superior to male students
in college examinations, and in no way behind
them in application, power of reasoning and
resource. The fact that much of their success
is due to the great interest taken in their
studies by their lecturers and professors is not
without a certain special significance. 
	
	The golden moments in the stream of life
rush past us, and we see nothing but sand; the
angels come to visit us, and we only know them 
when they are gone.— George Eliot.
	Run not into debt, either for wares sold or
money borrowed; be content to want things
that are not of absolute necessity rather than
to run up the score.—Sir M. Hale. 
	Our brains are seventy-year clocks. The
angel of life winds them up once for all, then
closes the case and gives the key into the hands
of the angel of the resurrection.
